chunks.text:
DRAFT Guidelines regarding the use of AI at Partners of the Americas (including ChatGPT, Bard, and other similar AI platforms)
For: Staff, consultants, interns
Artificial intelligence (AI) platforms such as ChatGPT, Bard, and others are becoming increasingly available and used in a variety of settings. AI can be used to generate ideas, brainstorm, draft content, summarize research, and more. It has been implicated in cheating when students use it for graded assignments; it is also being used as a teaching and learning tool in classrooms. 
In an organization like Partners of the Americas (Partners), AI can be a useful tool to aid in the writing of internal and external documents. However, we must be thoughtful and deliberate about its use. These guidelines outline our expectations for staff, consultants, and interns about the use of AI. 
Potential benefits of using AI: 
AI can provide first drafts of written material that may help get a task completed more quickly. 
AI may identify themes or data that would otherwise not have been considered by the writer.
AI may facilitate writing in different styles (academic, corporate, personal) to increase our ability to communicate with different audiences.
Recognized challenges and shortfalls of AI:
AI systems are trained on past information and may not have access to the latest research or analysis on a topic.
AI can be wrong; it will still give you an answer, even if incorrect.
AI does not know our organizational values or policies, and unless included in the prompt, may provide content inconsistent with our values/policies.
Requirements when using AI:
When representing Partners, it is our individual and collective responsibility to ensure that the use of AI tools and technologies in writing documents is done in a responsible, ethical, and transparent manner. Requirements for the use of AI in writing at Partners include:
Do not enter any confidential information, including donor names or staff details, into AI. For example, if you want ChatGPT to draft a thank you note to a donor, use a fake name in the prompt. “Thank Wonder Woman for her $500 donation to Partners of the Americas.”
Do not trust AI. Review, and review again. Any content that any staff, consultant, or intern uses that was generated or supported by AI must be thoroughly reviewed for correctness. You are accountable for the content you present on behalf of Partners.
AI content is not a primary source. If you are citing facts/data, you need primary sources which you will need to find and confirm.
Review content relative to our language guides and usage. AI may say “disabled people” but we say “people with disabilities.” You are accountable for language you use, not the AI.
Be aware of bias. ChatGPT and other AI systems are not trained in our DEI principles or the ethos of Partners.
Use detailed prompts. The more specific the prompt, the higher quality the result.
Expectations for disclosing the use of AI in any Partners written material:
Partners will ensure that we disclose the use of AI, including by citing the specific tool or technology used.
When using AI for an internal draft to other staff such as an email, simply note “content supported by ChatGPT search with prompt [x]” (e.g., Draft ethical guidelines for using ChatGPT at work.)
If used for a final external product, cite the AI and your prompts. For example: “Some sections of this blog post were drafted with support by ChatGPT, with the prompt Write a blog post about how International Volunteer Day is celebrated around the world.”
If used as a draft generator only (for example, for a thank you letter to a donor), no attribution is required.

chunks.text:
What should a ChatGPT or AI usage policy contain?
Here are some areas that a board of directors could consider when creating an AI policy for their workplace:
Data privacy and security: A policy should be put in place that outlines how the company will collect, store, and protect the data used by AI systems. This includes ensuring that only authorised personnel access data and that it is stored securely.
Bias and discrimination: AI systems can reflect and amplify human biases and prejudices. The policy should address how the company will ensure that AI systems do not discriminate against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics such as race, gender, or age.
Transparency and explainability: The policy should require that AI systems used in the workplace are transparent and explainable. This means that employees should be able to understand how AI decisions are made and why specific outcomes are generated.
Employee training: The policy should require that all employees who work with AI systems are trained on how to use them effectively and ethically. This includes understanding the limitations of the technology and the potential impact on their work.
Accountability and responsibility: The policy should clearly define who is responsible for AI systems’ decisions in the workplace. This includes holding individuals and departments accountable for the outcomes generated by AI systems.
Ethical considerations: The policy should address ethical concerns surrounding the use of AI in the workplace, such as the potential impact on employment and the ethical use of AI in decision-making.
Continuous monitoring and improvement: The policy should require ongoing monitoring and modification of AI systems used in the workplace to ensure that they are functioning as intended and are not causing unintended consequences.

chunks.text: Accountability Policy Effective May 1st, 2026 Enacted John McPhail President & CEO Table of Contents 1.Objective3 2.Scope and Applicability3 3.Accountability and Performance Framework3 3.1Joint Accountability3 3.2Performance Based Accountability Framework3 3.3Progressive Enforcement4 4.Positive Performance Recognition4 5.Corrective Actions4 5.1Repeated or Unresolved Findings5 6.Reset Mechanism6 7.Documentation and Tracking6 8.Policy Review and Updates6 Objective This policy establishes a structured accountability framework that links audit and spot check outcomes to individual and team performance. It is designed to: Reinforce compliance with organizational policies and donor requirements (including 2 CFR 200) Incentivize strong internal controls and high-quality documentation. Ensure timely and consistent corrective action for identified deficiencies. Promote a culture of shared responsibility and continuous improvement. This policy operates in conjunction with the Audit and Spot Check Policy and does not replace audit procedures or reporting requirements. Scope and Applicability This policy applies all staff, consultants, and authorized signatories involved in financial, procurement, human resources, and programmatic transactions. For a list of authorized signatories, reference Partners’ Delegation of Authority Policy. The Director of Grants and Contracts is responsible for issuing all spot check and audit reports in accordance with the Audit and Spot Check Policy, ensuring that findings and recommended actions are clearly documented. Based on these reports, the Director of Grants and Contracts will provide recommendations for applicable performance based incentives or corrective actions under this policy. The President & CEO retains final authority for reviewing and approving all performance based incentives and corrective measures, ensuring consistency, and fairness. Accountability and Performance Framework This section defines how compliance performance is

assessed and reinforced based on audit and spot check results. It establishes shared accountability among responsible individuals and ensures consistent application of both incentives and corrective actions. Joint Accountability All individuals who review, approve, or authorize transactions share equal responsibility for compliance. Accountability outcomes (both positive and corrective) will apply equally to all documented signatories on the document in review. Performance Based Accountability Framework Corrective actions and incentives will consider: The severity of findings (minor versus major), or the absence of findings The frequency and recurrence of findings, or sustained periods of compliant performance The timeliness, quality, and effectiveness of corrective actions, as well as proactive efforts to prevent future issues. Progressive Enforcement Corrective actions will follow a structured escalation model to ensure fairness, consistency, and opportunity for improvement. Positive Performance Recognition Partners will apply a performance based system of rewards and incentive based on audit and spot check outcomes. The framework follows a “three-strike” model. Three consecutive spot checks or audits resulting in no exceptions qualify as a “positive strike.” To reinforce strong compliance practices, the following incentives apply: Spot Check/Audit Performance (“Positive Strikes”) Upon three (3) consecutive positive strikes, all signatories involved will receive: One (1) additional paid comp day Sustained Compliance Excellence A full 12-month period with no audit or spot check findings will result in: One-time spot performance bonus of XX% of base salary OR One-time gift card or other non-monetary bonus Department-Level Recognition Departments with no major violations, and fewer than 3 minor findings across a full year may receive: Recognition during all-staff meetings OR Priority consideration for professional development or discretionary funding opportunities

Corrective Actions Partners will apply a structured system of corrective actions based on audit and spot check outcomes to address noncompliance and strengthen internal controls. The framework follows a “three-strike” model, where each identified minor violation constitutes one (1) strike applied to all relevant signatories. Whereas a major violation could immediately count as three (3) strikes. Reference the Audit and Spot Check Policy for description of minor versus major violations. First Strike Formal written warning issued and documented. Requires acknowledgment of the finding and applicable policy requirements. Implement corrective action plan. Second Strike Mandatory compliance retraining to be completed within thirty (30) days for policy/topic. Increased monitoring and/or secondary review of transactions for a defined period (may be required) Third Strike or Major Violation Actions may include (depending on nature and severity of issue(s): Placement on a formal Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) Impact on annual performance evaluation, which may include: Reduction in merit-based salary increase. Temporary ineligibility for promotion Immediate suspension of delegation of approval authority Mandatory retraining and/or targeted corrective action plan Disciplinary action, up to and including termination, in accordance with HR policies. Repeated or Unresolved Findings Failure to implement corrective actions within required timelines as defined in the Audit and Spot Check Policy, or recurrence of previously identified findings, may result in additional escalation beyond the standard three-strike process. Additional actions may include: Extended monitoring or oversight periods Additional audits and spot checks Further restriction of delegation of authority PIP Reset Mechanism To promote improvement and fairness: Strikes reset after 12 consecutive months Demonstrated improvement during monitoring periods may: Shorten PIP duration (if applicable) Restore approval

authority or level of approval authority sooner (if applicable) Documentation and Tracking All incentives and corrective actions must be formally documented to ensure transparency, consistency, and auditability. Documentation must clearly describe the basis for the decision, including the relevant audit or spot check results, the classification of findings (if applicable), and the rationale for the performance based incentive or corrective measure applied. Records will be maintained in the following systems: Personnel files: to document all disciplinary actions, performance improvement plans, recognition, and incentive awards. Compliance tracking systems: to link actions directly to specific audit or spot check results, track trends over time, and support reporting, and monitoring. Policy Review and Updates This policy must be reviewed at least every two years or sooner if regulations, donor rules, or organizational risk conditions change.

chunks.text: Audit and Spot Check Policy Effective May 1st, 2026 Enacted John McPhail President & CEO Table of Contents 1.Objective3 2.Scope and Applicability3 3.Spot Check Audit Frequency3 3.1Risk Level3 3.2Spot Check Frequency Based on Risk Level4 4.Procurement and Contract Testing Requirements4 4.1Procurement and Contract Risk Level Based Sampling5 4.2Expansion of Procurement and Contract Testing5 5.Expense Transaction Testing Requirements6 5.1Expense Risk Level Based Sampling6 5.2Expansion of Expense Testing7 6.Human Resources and Personnel Testing Requirements7 6.1HR Risk Level Based Sampling8 6.2Expansion of HR Testing9 7.Subrecipient Monitoring and Testing Requirements9 7.1Subrecipient Risk Level Based Sampling10 7.2Expansion of Subrecipient Testing10 8.Compliance Matrix and Award Decision Documentation Testing Requirements10 8.1Compliance Matrix and Award Decision Risk Level Based Sampling11 8.2Expansion of Compliance Matrix and Award Decision Testing11 9.Documentation and Results Reporting12 9.1Major Violation Reporting13 9.2Timeline for Corrective Action14 10.Policy Review and Updates14 Objective The policy establishes a robust, risk‑based framework for conducting spot checks and internal program audits across all Partners of the Americas (Partners) programs. The policy is designed to ensure compliance with 2 CFR 200 (Uniform Guidance), donor regulations, cooperative agreement requirements, and internationally recognized internal control and audit standards. It also strengthens prevention, early detection, and remediation of fraud, waste, abuse, and noncompliance. Scope and Applicability This policy applies to headquarters, country offices, field operations, subrecipients, contractors, and implementing partners administering Partners funding. All financial, procurement, contracts, payroll, assets, and programmatic transactions are subject to auditing. Partners Director of Grants & Contracts or their designee will be responsible for conducting spot checks and audits in

accordance with the policy below. Upon completion of each spot check and audit, a report will be sent to the results of the spot checks will be communicated via email to the President & CEO and the ELT member of the affected Department. Spot Check Audit Frequency Partners will apply a risk-based monitoring framework to the frequency of its spot checks and audits. Spot check audits will be conducted based on the risk level and dollar value of each project as outlined below. Risk Level Based on each project’s annual budget in USD, audit history, and operating context, a risk level will be determined. For a program to be classified in a category, it must meet at a minimum two of the criteria in the particular category. The basis for risk level is as follows: High Risk: Greater than $2M annual budget New Country where Partners has not recently operated (past 3 years) Prior findings High‑risk environment (high level of corruption locally, other concerns, etc.) Medium Risk: Between $500K–$2M annual budget New Project but in country where Partners already operates Stable environment Low Risk: Less than $500K New or Extended Project Strong compliance history and environment Spot Check Frequency Based on Risk Level Based on the ranking of each project in accordance with Section 3.1, the spot check will be conducted based on the following frequency: High Risk: Quarterly spot checks Medium Risk: Semi‑annual spot checks Low Risk: Annual spot check Random Audits can be conducted anytime based on Director of Compliance concerns, program or administration team request, or situations that arise which warrant a spot check. Procurement and Contract Testing Requirements Spot check and audit procedures must include detailed testing of procurement and contracting activities to confirm compliance with 2 CFR 200 procurement standards, donor requirements, and Partners internal procurement policies. Reviews must verify that procurements demonstrate: Full and open competition, or properly

documented justification for noncompetitive procurement in accordance with allowable circumstances. Appropriate procurement method and threshold application (micro-purchase, small purchase, competitive proposal, or sole source). Documented cost or price analysis for procurements exceeding the Simplified Acquisition Threshold or where otherwise required. Independent vendor selection and evaluation, free from conflicts of interest or undue influence. Signed conflict-of-interest form from staff involved in the procurement process, when applicable. Proper contractual documentation, including scope of work, period of performance, deliverables, payment terms, and required federal provisions. Evidence of receipt and acceptance of goods or services prior to payment authorization. Accurate financial coding and budget allowability consistent with the approved award budget. Vendor selection rationale is clearly documented and retained in the procurement file. Contracts and purchase orders include all required federal flow-down provisions, as applicable. Deliverables are received, inspected, and approved prior to payment. Payments align with contract terms, milestones, or deliverable acceptance (where it can be reviewed). Procurement and Contract Risk Level Based Sampling For the period under review, procurement testing must follow a tiered, risk-based approach. The tier approach for sampling is as follows: High-Value Transactions 100% review is required for all procurements with a total value greater than $100,000 USD. Moderate-Value Transactions A risk-based sample must be selected from procurements below $100,000 USD but above the micro-purchase threshold (based on procurement policy for home and field office limits) The minimum sample size must equal the greater of: 10% of those transactions, or Five transactions per review period,unless a higher sample size is warranted based on risk indicators (e.g., repeat vendors, split purchases, or weak documentation). Low-Value

Transactions Transactions below the micro-purchase threshold (which is determined by the procurement policy for both home and field office) must be tested using random sampling sufficient to achieve at least 5% coverage of those transactions for the review period. Expansion of Procurement and Contract Testing If procurement noncompliance, missing competition, conflicts of interest, or unsupported costs are identified: Testing must be expanded to additional procurements and vendors. Questioned costs must be calculated and documented. Major violations as defined in Section 9, must be escalated to the President & CEO immediately within 1 business day. All other minor findings will be included in the report provided to the President & CEO. Expense Transaction Testing Requirements Spot checks must include detailed testing of expense transactions to confirm compliance with donor regulations, organizational policy, and generally accepted internal control standards. Spot checks must verify allowability, allocability, reasonableness, documentation, approvals, and correct budget coding of expenditures. Testing must verify that transactions are: Allowable under applicable donor rules, including 2 CFR 200 Subpart E and award-specific restrictions. Allocable to the benefiting program or cost objective. Reasonable and necessary for performance of the award. Accurately supported by complete, accurate, and properly retained source documentation. Approved in accordance with delegated authority and segregation-of-duties requirements. Correctly recorded and coded to the appropriate general ledger account, funding source, and budget line. Recognized in the proper accounting period and consistent with revenue recognition requirements, where applicable. Expense Risk Level Based Sampling For the period under review, the transaction testing must be performed using a risk-based, tiered sampling approach designed to provide sufficient audit evidence while prioritizing higher-risk

expenditures. The tier approach for sampling is as follows: High-Value Transactions 100% review is required for all individual expense or revenue transactions greater than $50,000 USD. For subrecipients it is for expense reports over $50,000 USD average monthly Moderate-Value Transactions A risk-based sample must be selected from transactions between $10,000 and $99,999 USD. The minimum sample size must equal the greater of: 25% of those transactions, or Five transactions per cost category,unless a higher sample size is warranted based on risk indicators (e.g., new vendor, prior findings, unusual timing, split purchases, or weak controls). For subrecipients it is for expense reports over $25,000 USD average monthly Low-Value Transactions Transactions below $10,000 USD must be tested using random sampling sufficient to achieve at least 10% coverage of those transactions for the review period. For subrecipients it is for expense reports under $25,000 USD average monthly Expansion of Expense Testing If material errors, unsupported costs, internal control failures, or indicators of fraud are identified: The sample size must be expanded immediately until the extent of risk is understood. Questioned costs must be quantified and documented. Major violations as defined in Section 9, must be escalated to the President & CEO immediately within 1 business day. All other minor findings will be included in the report provided to the President & CEO. Human Resources and Personnel Testing Requirements Spot checks must include detailed testing of human resources processes, personnel files, and employee-related transactions to confirm compliance with donor regulations, organizational policies, labor laws, and generally accepted internal control standards. Testing must ensure that hiring, compensation, benefits, and employee-related procurements are properly authorized, documented, and compliant with Partners salary scale. Testing must verify that: Recruitment and hiring processes

are conducted in accordance with organizational policy, including proper approvals, competitive selection (where required), and documentation of hiring decisions. Employee files are complete, accurate, and securely maintained, including employment agreements, job descriptions, salary history, and required compliance documentation. Compensation, benefits, and allowances are authorized, reasonable, and consistent with approved salary scales, donor regulations, and local labor laws. Changes in employment status are documented (e.g., promotions, salary adjustments, terminations). This include ensuring properly approved and documented paperwork. Segregation of duties is maintained across hiring, payroll processing, and approval functions. Consultants and individual contractors are properly classified and not treated as employees in violation of applicable regulations. Employee equipment documented (e.g., equipment issued to staff) are properly authorized, supported, and tracked. Required background checks, conflict-of-interest disclosures, and other compliance requirements are completed and documented, where applicable. HR Risk Level Based Sampling For the period under review, human resources testing must be performed using a risk-based, tiered sampling approach designed to provide sufficient audit evidence while prioritizing higher-risk personnel actions and transactions. The tier approach for sampling is as follows: High-Risk Personnel Actions 100% review is required for: Senior leadership hires and terminations (Grade D in the salary scale and above) Salary adjustments or bonuses exceeding $10,000 USD annually, or 10% of the salary, whichever is lower New senior level hires (top two tiers) in high-risk environments or countries where Partners has not recently operated programs (no recent projects in last 3 years) Any employees or consultants with prior findings or identified risks Moderate-Risk Personnel Actions A risk-based sample must be selected from: Standard new

hires, contract renewals, and salary adjustments The minimum sample size must equal 25% of those transactions or personnel files, or a minimum of five (5) employee files per review period: Unless a higher sample size is warranted based on risk indicators (e.g., high turnover, decentralized hiring, prior findings, or weak HR controls). Low-Risk Personnel Actions Remaining employee files and HR transactions must be tested using random sampling sufficient to achieve at least 10% coverage of personnel files for the review period. Expansion of HR Testing If noncompliance, internal control weaknesses, payroll errors, misclassification of employees, or indicators of fraud are identified: The sample size must be expanded immediately until the extent of risk is understood. Questioned costs must be quantified and documented. Major violations as defined in Section 9, must be escalated to the President & CEO immediately within 1 business day. All other minor findings will be included in the report provided to the President & CEO. Subrecipient Monitoring and Testing Requirements Partners is implementing a risk-based subrecipient monitoring framework to ensure that all subawards are administered in compliance with 2 CFR 200, donor requirements, and the terms and conditions of each subaward agreement. Monitoring activities must provide reasonable assurance that: Subrecipient expenditures are allowable, allocable, reasonable, and properly supported in accordance with 2 CFR 200 Subpart E. Programmatic performance and deliverables are achieved in accordance with the approved scope of work and performance indicators, Financial management systems and internal controls meet minimum federal standards. Required audits under 2 CFR 200 Subpart F are completed, reviewed, and resolved. Subaward terms, special conditions, and reporting requirements are fully enforced. For pre-award and annual risk assessments, please see Partners full Risk Assessment Subrecipient vs Contractor Policy.

Subrecipient Risk Level Based Sampling Subrecipient transaction testing must follow a tiered, value-based methodology applied to the total annual subaward value for the period under review. High-Value Transactions 100% review is required for one month’s expense report Where invoice volume is high, sampling may be applied 60% of total invoiced value. Moderate-Value Transactions 50% review is required for one month’s expense report Low-Value Transactions 25% review is required for one month’s expense report Expansion of Subrecipient Testing If procurement noncompliance, missing competition, conflicts of interest, or unsupported costs are identified: Expand testing to additional invoices or the full population, as appropriate. Questioned costs must be calculated and documented. Major violations as defined in Section 9, must be escalated to the President & CEO immediately within 1 business day. All other minor findings will be included in the report provided to the President & CEO. Compliance Matrix and Award Decision Documentation Testing Requirements Spot checks must include detailed testing of compliance matrices and award decision-making documentation to confirm compliance with donor regulations, organizational policies, and procurement standards. Testing must ensure that funding decisions and award justifications are transparent, properly documented, and aligned with applicable requirements. Testing must verify that: A complete and accurate compliance matrix is maintained for each award and is updated. Approval from donors is readily available and documented in writing. Any deviations from standard procedures are documented with donor approval (e.g., sole source, limited competition, budget changes, program changes/waivers, etc.). Required approvals for award decisions are obtained from the Grants/Agreement individual authorized to provide approval (e.g. approval for subrecipients, key personnel, M&E plans, workplans, disposition plans, vehicles, equipment

purchases, etc.). Award decisions are compliant with 2 CFR 200 and any donor-specific requirements. Compliance Matrix and Award Decision Risk Level Based Sampling For the period under review, testing must be performed using a risk-based, tiered sampling approach designed to provide sufficient audit evidence while prioritizing higher-risk award decisions and approvals. The tier approach for sampling is as follows: High Risk: Greater than $2M annual budget New Country where Partners has not recently operated (past 3 years) Prior findings High‑risk environment (high level of corruption locally, other concerns, etc.) Medium Risk: Between $500K–$2M annual budget New Project but in country where Partners already operates Stable environment Low Risk: Less than $500K New or Extended Project Strong compliance history and environment Expansion of Compliance Matrix and Award Decision Testing If inconsistencies in documentation, undocumented award decisions, noncompliance with donor standards, or indicators of fraud are identified: The sample size must be expanded immediately until the extent of risk is understood. Questioned costs must be quantified and documented. Major violations as defined in Section 9, must be escalated to the President & CEO immediately within 1 business day. All other minor findings will be included in the report provided to the President & CEO. Documentation and Results Reporting All spot check audit testing must be fully documented, reviewable, and retained in a manner that provides sufficient, appropriate audit evidence to support conclusions, questioned costs, and corrective actions. Documentation and reporting must be clear enough that an independent reviewer could understand the work performed, evidence obtained, and basis for conclusions without additional explanation. The results of the spot checks will be communicated via email to the President & CEO and the ELT member of the affected Department, excepting issues pertaining directly to the

latter. Upon review of the reports, appropriate communications and actions will then be communicated to the corresponding Department team members. The role of the compliance team is not to enforce corrective action, but rather to identify corrective actions that will be enforced through the appropriate level within the affected Department. The email report will include the following: Review identification Program, country, or subrecipient reviewed Review period covered Scope and methodology summary Functional areas reviewed (e.g., expenses, procurement, and contracts, subrecipient invoices) Sampling approach and number of items tested Overall conclusion Statement indicating whether testing found: No exceptions, or Minor issues addressed through informal follow-up Summary of minor violations (if applicable) Brief description of the issue Corrective action requested Summary of major violations (if applicable) Major violations identified during spot checks or audits require immediate escalation, and corrective action due to their potential financial, legal, and reputational impact on Partners. See Section 9.1 for major violation reporting. A major violation includes, but is not limited to: Indicators of fraud, corruption, or intentional misrepresentation Material questioned costs or systemic unallowable expenditures Significant internal control failures or breakdown in segregation of duties Unauthorized procurements, conflicts of interest, or noncompliance with competition requirements Falsified, altered, or missing supporting documentation Repeated or unresolved findings from prior audits Noncompliance that could result in donor disallowances, penalties, or award termination The reports will be issued via email or memorandum. The final version will be saved in the official compliance file of the project. Major Violation Reporting Upon identification of a major violation, the following actions must be taken immediately: The Director of Compliance must notify the

President & CEO within 1 business day. Immediate action will be taken based on the severity, and may include: Affected transactions, payments, procurements, or subrecipient disbursements may be temporarily suspended pending review. Access to financial systems or delegated authorities may be restricted. A documented investigation must be conducted, which may include expanded testing and interviews with relevant personnel. All questioned costs must be fully quantified and documented. An assessment must be made regarding potential financial exposure, donor reporting obligations, and legal implications. A formal corrective action plan must be developed and implemented immediately. Disciplinary measures, up to and including termination of staff, contractors, or subrecipient agreements, may be applied as appropriate. If required in accordance with 2 CFR 200 and award terms, material violations must be reported to the donor in a timely manner, in coordination with the President & CEO. The affected program, office, or subrecipient will be subject to increased risk classification and more frequent spot checks or audits. A major violation is only considered closed once all corrective actions, recoveries, and required reporting have been completed and verified. Timeline for Corrective Action Partners expect that all spot check findings will be resolved timely and in compliance with 2 CFR 200. The Program or Administrative Unit Director is expected to respond to the results within 5 business days via email and communicate a corrective action plan. The corrective action plan must be completed within 60 days of the spot check finding. Note for major violations as defined in Section 9, the timeline may require immediate response and correction if indicated by the Director of Compliance. In coordination with the Department ELT member, the Director of Compliance will conduct a follow-up to verify corrective actions taken. An issue is considered closed only after full corrective

action is verified and documented. If a finding is not resolved within the 60-day period, the Director of Compliance will escalate to the President & CEO. Policy Review and Updates This policy must be reviewed at least every two years or sooner if regulations, donor rules, or organizational risk conditions change.

chunks.heading: PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING PROTECTION POLICY PLEDGE
chunks.text:
As a member of this Partners of the Americas (Partners) program, I pledge to do the following at all times with all participants:
I commit to appropriate calm and respectful behavior.  
I will be clear about the standards of behavior I expect from participants and all adults associated with the program.
In the case of anger and disagreements between participants I will stop all activities and mediate a resolution calmly; using examples of life skills applied in the program.
I will not ignore any participant or refuse to allow them access to basic necessities.
I will never punish a participant by mocking, teasing, or laughing at them or trying to embarrass them in front of others
I will not behave sexually towards any participant or touch them in an inappropriate sexual manner
I will not hit or strike any participant as a punishment for inappropriate behavior
I will know where to get assistance for any injury and help to minimize further harm or infection as a result of an injury. 
I commit to providing a safe and appropriate physical environment for the participants in our program throughout all phases of the project.

As a member of the Partners program, I agree to help to protect myself and other people in these ways:
1. I understand that I should be able to take part in all program phases safely
2. I will tell an adult I trust if anything worries me
3. I will respect myself and behave respectfully – politely, considerately and thoughtfully - towards other people
4. I will keep calm if there is a disagreement
5. I understand that others are not permitted to hit or abuse me. 
6. I understand I am to act appropriately and not hurt other people. 
7. I understand that I should not be bullied. I will not bully other people.
7. I understand that other people should not shout at me to upset, scare or intimidate me. I will not shout at other people.
8. I understand that my sexual parts (breasts, bottom, or genitals) should not be touched by other
people. I should not act in a sexual way towards myself or other participants or program staff.
9. I understand that if any physical force is used by adults it should only be used to secure my safety or that of other participants. 
10. I will tell anything that concerns or worries me to an adult I trust.

chunks.text:
Partners of the Americas Code of Conduct
No code or policy can anticipate every situation that may arise. Accordingly, this Code is intended to serve as a source of guiding principles. Partners of the Americas reserves the right to make all decisions on a case-by-case basis. 
Introduction
Each employee, volunteer, representative and member of the Partners Network is expected to adhere to a high standard of ethical conduct. The good name of Partners of the Americas depends on the way its network and representatives conduct business and the way the public perceives that conduct. Unethical actions, or the appearance of unethical actions, are not acceptable. 
Confidentiality
All persons interacting with or representing the Partners Network should maintain the confidentiality of information entrusted to them by Partners of the Americas and any other confidential information about the organization, its activities, partners, funders, stakeholders or suppliers, that comes to them, from whatever source, except when disclosure is authorized or legally mandated. 
International Activity
Partners of the Americas conducts operations and activities around the world. Many of the countries where Partners conducts business have laws and regulations that may be significantly different from those of the US. Persons interacting with or representing Partners in a foreign country are responsible for knowing and complying with these laws. Furthermore, anyone interacting with or representing the Partners Network must also take care to understand and respect the cultures and customs of the countries where Partners operates.
Anyone working in the international arena should be aware of the US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act. The Act prohibits giving anything of value to officials of foreign governments in order to obtain or retain business. It is prohibited for any representative of Partners of the Americas or its network to engage in bribery, kickbacks, payoffs or other corrupt business practices. It is also Partners of the Americas’ policy to fully comply with all applicable US export, customs and trade control and regulations, licensing/ trademarking/ copyright requirements and other relevant US and international laws. 
Harassment
Partners of the Americas intends to provide a pleasant, healthful, comfortable, and professional atmosphere that is free from intimidation, hostility or other offenses which might interfere with the pursuit of our mission. Moreover, Partners of the Americas does not tolerate discrimination on the basis of sex, race, color, age, religion, disability, national origin, sexual orientation, or any other legally prohibited characteristic. Harassment or discrimination based on sex or any other legally prohibited basis is illegal and is contrary to the policy of Partners of the Americas. 
Compliance
If you know of or suspect a violation of applicable laws, rules or regulations or this Code, you must immediately report that information to any Senior Manager or member of the International Board of Partners of the Americas, Inc. The President or Chairman of the Board shall determine, or shall designate appropriate persons to determine, appropriate action in response to violations of this Code.
Partners of the Americas prohibits any retaliation against anyone who makes a good faith report of a suspected violation or who participates in an investigation. There is to be no interference, coercion, discrimination, reprisal or retaliation against any person for making a valid and truthful complaint or good faith report of a violation. 
Nothing in this policy is meant to, nor should it be interpreted to, in any way limit your rights under any applicable federal, state, or local laws.
By signing below I acknowledge that I have read and agree to this policy.
Printed Name:________________________________________________________________________
Signature:Date:_______________

chunks.heading: UNITED STATES YOUTH PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING AND PROTECTION POLICY
chunks.text: Purpose/Goal Partners of the Americas and its affiliates (referred to as “Partners”) are committed to providing a safe and nurturing environment for all children or youth who participate in events sponsored by the organization. This policy is intended to provide guidelines and best practices for promoting and maintaining a safe and secure environment in all interactions with children or youth while assuring all representatives of the Partners of the Americas are acting responsibly to protect children or youth. The goal of this policy is to provide an environment that is safe and secure for participants in all Partners sponsored outreach events, programs, and activities, wherever they occur. It further seeks to minimize the possibility for physical and sexual abuse of children or youth. Compliance All of those listed in the section below are required to comply with this policy. Partners affirms this policy and will not tolerate any abuse. This policy does not preclude the reporting of suspected abuse occurring outside of this policy and it applies to all groups, activities and event sponsored by Partners as well as groups using Partners’ facilities who serve youth. Those who must comply with this policy The following people must comply with the Children or Youth Safeguarding Policy: All staff, full time, part time, international and national, and those engaged on short-term contracts, e.g. consultants, facilitators, mentors, sub-contractors, researchers etc. (referred to as “staff”); Volunteers, board members, trustees (referred to as “representatives”); Staff and representatives of partner agencies and any other individuals, groups or organizations who have a formal/contractual relationship to Partners of the Americas that involves them having contact with children or youth - unless it has been agreed under the global guidance and local procedures that the partner organization may enforce its own safeguarding or protection policy (referred to as “staff of

chunks.heading: UNITED STATES YOUTH PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING AND PROTECTION POLICY
chunks.text: implementing organization”) Definition of children or youth Children or youth under this policy is anyone under the age of 18. Definition of Abuse Abuse is defined as any sexual contact, indecent exposure, or communication for immoral purposes as well as physical abuses such as corporal punishment or physical assault. Types of Abuse 1. Physical abuse is non-accidental injury, which is intentionally inflicted upon a child or youth. 2. Sexual abuse perpetrated by an adult is any contact or activity of a sexual nature that occurs between a child or youth and an adult. This includes any activity, which is meant to arouse or gratify the sexual desires of the adult, child, or youth. 3. Sexual abuse perpetrated by another child or youth is any contact or activity of a sexual nature that occurs between a child or youth and another child or youth when there is no consent, when consent is not possible, or when one child or youth has power over the other child or youth. This includes any activity which is meant to arouse or gratify the sexual desires of any of the children or youth. 4. Emotional abuse is mental or emotional injury to a child or youth that results in an observable and material impairment in the child or youth’s growth, development or psychological functioning. 5. Neglect is the failure to provide for a child or youth's basic needs or the failure to protect a child or youth from harm. 6. Economic exploitation is the deliberate misplacement, exploitation, or wrongful temporary or permanent use of a child or youth's belongings or money. Responsibilities, Procedures and Practices Responsibilities Personnel are prohibited from the use, possession, distribution, or being under the influence of alcohol, illegal drugs, or the misuse of legal drugs while participating in or assisting with programs or activities specifically for children or youth. 2. Parents or guardians must complete written permission forms before Personnel transport children and youth for a company

chunks.heading: UNITED STATES YOUTH PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING AND PROTECTION POLICY
chunks.text: sponsored activity or for any purpose on more than an occasional basis. 3. Personnel will respond to children and youth with respect, consideration and equal treatment, regardless of sex, race, religion, sexual orientation, culture or socio- economic status. 4. Personnel will portray a positive role model for children and youth by maintaining an attitude of respect, patience, and maturity. They will avoid even the appearance of favoritism. 5. One-to-one counseling with children or youth will be done in an open or public or other place where private conversations are possible but occur in full view of others. 6. Personnel are prohibited from dating or becoming romantically involved with a child or youth. 7. Personnel are prohibited from having sexual contact with a child or youth. 8. Personnel are prohibited from possessing any sexually oriented materials (magazines, cards, videos, films, clothing etc.) on organization property or in the presence of children or youth except as expressly permitted as part of a pre-authorized educational program. 9. Personnel are prohibited from using the Internet to view or download any sexually oriented materials on company property or in the presence of children or youth. 10. Personnel are prohibited from discussing their own sexual activities, including dreams and fantasies, or discussing their use of sexually oriented or explicit materials such as pornography, videos or materials on or from the Internet, with children or youth. 11. Personnel are prohibited from sleeping in the same beds, sleeping bags, tents, hotel rooms or other rooms with children or youth unless the adult is an immediate family member of all children or youth in the bed, sleeping bag, tent, hotel room or other room. It is acceptable to have multiple adults sleep with all the children or youth participating in one open space such as a camp lodge. 12. Personnel are prohibited from dressing, undressing, bathing, or showering in the presence of children or youth.

chunks.heading: UNITED STATES YOUTH PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING AND PROTECTION POLICY
chunks.text: 13. Personnel are prohibited from using physical punishment in any way for behavior management of children and youth. No form of physical discipline is acceptable. This prohibition includes spanking, slapping, pinching, hitting, or any other physical force. Physical force may only be used to stop a behavior that may cause immediate harm to the individual or to a child, youth or others. Procedures Procedures continued Every program for children and youth must have established ratios of 2 adults per 20 children. Compliance with the established ratio is required at all times, including activities that occur off the organization’s premises. 2. Personnel are prohibited from being alone with a child or youth or multiple children or youth where other adults cannot easily observe them. 3. Youth under the age of 18 must be must directly supervised by Personnel. The Personnel must be physically present during all activities. 4. Personnel are not permitted to develop new activities for children and youth without approval from the director. Requests to develop new activities should be submitted in writing to the director. The director will consider whether the plan for a new activity is appropriate for the age group and if it includes adequate adult supervision. 5. Each program will develop age-appropriate procedures to ensure the safety of children and youth using restrooms and showers or baths. 6. When supervising or assisting private activities such as dressing or showering children or youth, Personnel will remain in an area observable by other adults or work in pairs. 7. When both boys and girls are participating, male and female adults must be present. Screening and Selection Personnel who regularly work with or around children or youth Shall be screened and selected utilizing at least the following: a. A STANDARD APPLICATION completed by the applicant that includes an authorization for the release of information to conduct background checks and the CODE OF CONDUCT

chunks.heading: UNITED STATES YOUTH PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING AND PROTECTION POLICY
chunks.text: (Appendix C). b. CRIMINAL RECORDS CHECK in any state where the applicant has resided during the past seven (7) years, and other states, if any, as determined by the company. c. SEXUAL OFFENDER REGISTRY CHECK in any state where the applicant has resided during the past seven (7) years. d. INDIVIDUAL INTERVIEW with the applicant. e. REFERENCE CHECKS of persons outside the organization who know the applicant, preferably who know how the applicant works with children. f. DRIVING OR MOTOR VEHICLE RECORDS CHECK if the person will be transporting Personnel who occasionally work with or Around Children or youth Shall be screened and selected utilizing at least the following: a. A STANDARD APPLICATION completed by the applicant that includes an acknowledgment for the release of information to conduct background checks and the CODE OF CONDUCT (Appendix C). b. INDIVIDUAL INTERVIEW with the applicant. c. AT LEAST ONE REFERENCE CHECK of a person or persons outside of Partners of the Americas who know the applicant, preferably who know how the applicant interacts with children. d. REFERENCE CHECKS of persons outside the organization who know the applicant, preferably who know how the applicant works with children. e. DRIVING OR MOTOR VEHICLE RECORDS CHECK if the person will be transporting Requirements Prior to interacting with children or youth, the following steps need to be taken: Complete Application(Appendix D) Attend training every years Education and Training Requirements Education and Training Requirements continued Training will be provided to each and every person who will teach, work with, counsel, supervise, chaperone, lead or direct any participant, or volunteer under the age of 18. Two hours of child abuse prevention education and training is required for all PERSONNEL WHO REGULARLY WORK WITH OR AROUND CHILDREN OR YOUTH before they start their work with children or youth or, if that is not possible, one hour of child abuse awareness training before they start their

chunks.heading: UNITED STATES YOUTH PARTICIPANT SAFEGUARDING AND PROTECTION POLICY
chunks.text:
work and the rest of the training within three months of starting. One hour of child abuse awareness education and training is required for all

YOUTH before they start their work with children or youth.
Personnel who are responsible for screening, selection and supervision of others in programs for children and youth are required to complete an additional two (2) hours of specialized education and training in screening, selection and monitoring every two years.
Re-training will be required every two years to continue service with youth. 
Directors of each Partners program are required to:
      - Present Youth Participant Safeguarding and Protection Policy Training
      - Maintain individual confidential records that include:
Who has attended training and the date of attendance
Process the 2 referrals for those who attended the training
Review for completeness and compliance, the initial and                                                                                     updated(every two years) applications
Responding to Problems
A. REPORTING INAPPROPRIATE BEHAVIORS OR POLICY VIOLATIONS WITH

chunks.heading: CHILDREN OR YOUTH
chunks.text: 1. When Personnel observe any inappropriate behaviors, behaviors that are inconsistent with the GUIDELINES FOR APPROPRIATE AFFECTION, or which may violate any provision of these Policies the for Protection of Children and Youth from Abuse, they must immediately report their observations. Examples of inappropriate behaviors or policy violations would be seeking private time with children or youth, taking children or youth on over-night trips without other adults, swearing or making suggestive comments to children or youth, or selecting or using staff or volunteers without the required screening. 2. Such inappropriate behaviors or possible policy violations that relate to interactions with children or youth should be reported in one of the following ways: a. A telephone call/e-mail or meeting with the immediate supervisor of the person and notification to Human Resources; b. Submit a NOTICE OF CONCERN (Appendix F), signed or unsigned, to the Chief Operating Officer, Horacio Correa at hcorrea@partners.net. 3. All reports of inappropriate behavior or policy violations with children or youth will be taken seriously. Communication If there is an incident or tragedy in a country or program you support, you are not to send communications, including e-mails, to employees, donors, participants or others involved in the program about the incident. “Incident” as defined here includes not cover just instances of abuse but also violence, suicide, accident, or other trauma. All details concerning the incident are to be provided to the Communications Unit of Partners and they will issue communication to the appropriate parties. Due to the sensitivity of the subject matter, all communications must be approved by the Communications Unit. This ESPECIALLY includes, but is not limited to, communications with the general public or the media. Communications will develop all necessary documents or releases with an eye to minimizing harm to the child/ children involved. This may involve

chunks.heading: CHILDREN OR YOUTH
chunks.text: taking measures to protect the identity of the child/ children. Communications will adhere strictly to best practices of journalism as they relate toward interacting with child victims and their parents, including informed consent. Communications may interact with Senior Management, as well as legal and human resources counsel, in the development for materials for any of the above described situations. Appropriate interaction with Children Some POSITIVE and APPROPRIATE forms of affection are listed below: Brief hugs. Pats on the shoulder or back. Handshakes. "High-fives" and hand slapping. Verbal praise. Arms around shoulders. Kneeling or bending down for hugs with small children. Inappropriate interaction with Children Inappropriate interaction with Children continued The following are forms of INAPPROPRIATE behavior with children and youth. Inappropriate or lengthy embraces. Kisses on the mouth. Touching bottoms, chests or genital areas. Holding hands Showing affection in isolated areas such as bedrooms, closets, staff only areas or other private rooms. Occupying a bed with a child or youth. Dancing Touching knees or legs of children or youth. Wrestling with children or youth. Tickling children or youth. Piggyback rides. Any type of massage given by a child or youth to an adult. Any type of massage given by an adult to a child or youth. Any form of unwanted affection. Comments or compliments (spoken, written, or electronic) that relate to physique or body development. Examples would be, "You sure are developing," or "You look really hot in those jeans." Snapping bras or giving wedgies or similar touch of underwear whether or not it is covered by other clothing. Giving gifts or money to individual children or youth. Private meals with individual children or youth. chunks.text: Code de conduite pour la protection de l'enfance Aucun code de conduite ou politique ne peut prévoir toutes les situations qui peuvent se présenter. Par conséquent, ce code est destiné à servir de source de principes directeurs et à compléter les politiques déjà établies, et non à constituer une liste exhaustive. Les personnes qui travaillent dans le cadre de récompenses ayant leur propre code de conduite à ce sujet doivent également respecter ces codes. Partners of the Americas (POA) se réserve le droit de prendre toutes les décisions au cas par cas. Sauvegarde des enfants La POA s'engage à fournir un environnement de travail sûr et stimulant et à protéger les enfants qui participent aux programmes et/ou aux événements parrainés par l'organisation. POA s'engage à soutenir les victimes, à améliorer la capacité de sauvegarde et à garantir la sécurité des enfants lorsqu'ils participent à ses programmes et/ou aux événements parrainés par l'organisation. La protection de l'enfance consiste en des actions préventives visant à garantir que tous les enfants sont protégés contre les actes délibérés ou involontaires qui entraînent un risque de préjudice ou un préjudice réel de la part des affiliés de POA qui entrent en contact avec les enfants ou qui ont un impact sur eux dans le cadre de nos interventions de développement, de nos réponses humanitaires et de nos opérations. En outre, POA ne tolérera aucune violation de cette politique de protection des enfants et confirme son engagement à signaler, enquêter et répondre à toute violation présumée de la politique. POA adopte une approche de tolérance zéro à l'égard de l'exploitation et/ou de la maltraitance des enfants par tout affilié de POA. Cette politique s'applique à tous les affiliés de POA qui interagissent avec des enfants. Les programmes actuels de POA soutiennent des activités avec des enfants, qui sont particulièrement vulnérables aux abus et à l'exploitation. Ceci est dû à la différence de pouvoir inhérente qui

existe entre les organisations d'aide et les communautés touchées, ce qui augmente le risque d'abus et d'exploitation. Afin de protéger tous les bénéficiaires des programmes contre cette différence de pouvoir, la politique de protection des enfants, la politique de prévention de l'exploitation et des abus sexuels et la politique de harcèlement sexuel servent à transformer les valeurs de POA en comportements auxquels tous les affiliés de POA sont censés se conformer. La capacité de POA à réaliser sa vision et sa mission dépend de tous les affiliés de POA. Par conséquent, tous les affiliés de POA doivent respecter et promouvoir les normes les plus élevées en matière de conduite éthique et professionnelle. La POA interdit tout acte de maltraitance, d'exploitation ou de négligence envers les enfants, d'exploitation économique, d'intimidation et de cyberintimidation, ou de mauvais traitement. Les principes fondamentaux suivants doivent être respectés par tous les affiliés de la POA : Tous les enfants de moins de 18 ans ont un droit égal à la protection contre toutes les formes de violence, comme le déclare l'article 19 de la Convention des Nations Unies relative aux droits de l'enfant. Les droits de l'homme des enfants seront respectés et appliqués à tous, indépendamment de l'âge, du sexe, du genre, de l'identité sexuelle, de l'orientation sexuelle, de la nationalité, de l'ethnie, de la couleur, de la race, de la langue, des croyances religieuses ou politiques, de l'état civil, du handicap, de la santé physique ou mentale, de la famille, du milieu socio-économique ou culturel, de la classe sociale ou de tout autre aspect de leur origine ou de leur identité. L'inégalité, l'exclusion et la discrimination seront contestées et ne seront pas tolérées ; Veiller au respect de la législation du pays d'accueil et de la législation locale en matière de bien-être et de protection de l'enfance ou des normes internationales, selon ce qui offre la plus grande protection, et de la

législation américaine, le cas échéant ; Interdire à tout le personnel de se livrer à la maltraitance, l'exploitation ou la négligence des enfants ; Tenir compte de la protection de l'enfance dans la planification et la mise en œuvre du projet afin de déterminer les risques potentiels pour les enfants qui sont associés aux activités et aux opérations du projet et identifier les lacunes et les moyens d'y remédier ; Appliquer des mesures visant à réduire le risque d'abus, d'exploitation ou de négligence à l'égard des enfants, y compris, mais sans s'y limiter, en limitant les interactions non supervisées avec les enfants, en interdisant l'exposition à la pornographie et en se conformant aux lois, règlements ou coutumes applicables concernant la photographie, le tournage ou toute autre activité génératrice d'images d'enfants ; Promouvoir des procédures de contrôle de la sécurité des enfants pour le personnel, en particulier le personnel dont le travail le met en contact direct avec les enfants ; Disposer d'une procédure permettant de s'assurer que le personnel et les autres personnes reconnaissent les cas de maltraitance, d'exploitation ou de négligence d'enfants, d'exiger que le personnel et les autres personnes signalent les allégations, d'enquêter et de gérer les allégations et de prendre les mesures appropriées en réponse à ces allégations, y compris, mais sans s'y limiter, le licenciement du personnel ; Former tous les affiliés de la POA pour qu'ils sachent comment signaler et qui contacter en cas de problème, aussi petit ou insignifiant qu'il puisse paraître ; Répondre aux préoccupations, y compris le mandat d'enquêter sur toutes les allégations crédibles de violation de la présente politique de protection de l'enfance. Les affiliés de la POA sont tenus de coopérer à toute enquête ultérieure. Des mesures correctives, y compris toute mesure personnelle ou disciplinaire, seront prises pour empêcher la répétition d'une telle activité ; et Veiller à ce que tous les

affiliés de la POA reçoivent une formation et soient sensibilisés aux attentes de la présente politique afin de prévenir, de signaler et de répondre aux problèmes de protection. En outre, il faut s'assurer que tous les bénéficiaires du programme connaissent les normes de comportement auxquelles ils peuvent s'attendre de la part des affiliés de la POA et la façon dont ils peuvent soulever une préoccupation ou signaler une violation présumée. Les affiliés de la POA sont tenus de respecter le code de conduite suivant lorsqu'ils interagissent avec des enfants : Tout individu âgé de moins de 18 ans est un enfant et est "mineur", quel que soit l'âge légal de consentement du pays dans lequel l'enfant vit et/ou dans lequel l'infraction se produit ; Les affiliés de la POA ne doivent pas maltraiter ou exploiter un enfant ou se comporter d'une manière qui met un enfant en danger ; Les activités sexuelles avec des enfants (définis par la Convention relative aux droits de l'enfant comme toutes les personnes âgées de moins de 18 ans) sont interdites quel que soit l'âge de la majorité ou l'âge du consentement au niveau local. La croyance erronée en l'âge d'un enfant ne constitue pas une défense ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de sortir avec un enfant ou d'avoir une relation amoureuse avec lui ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'utiliser un langage ou de se comporter à l'égard d'un enfant ou d'un jeune d'une manière inappropriée, offensante, abusive, sexuellement provocante, dégradante ou culturellement inadaptée ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de caresser, de tenir, d'embrasser, de serrer dans leurs bras ou de toucher les enfants d'une manière inappropriée ou culturellement insensible ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'agir de manière à faire honte aux enfants et aux jeunes, à les humilier, à les rabaisser ou à les dégrader, ou à perpétrer toute autre forme de violence psychologique ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de faire des choses de

nature personnelle pour les enfants avec lesquels ils sont en contact dans un contexte professionnel (par exemple, accompagner un enfant/un jeune aux toilettes/à la salle de bain ; l'aider à se déshabiller) ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de dormir dans les mêmes lits, sacs de couchage, tentes, chambres d'hôtel ou autres pièces avec des enfants, sauf si l'adulte est un membre de la famille immédiate de tous les enfants dans le lit, le sac de couchage, la tente, la chambre d'hôtel ou autre pièce. Il est acceptable que plusieurs adultes dorment avec tous les enfants participant dans un espace ouvert, tel qu'un pavillon de camp. En outre, il est interdit aux affiliés de la LPO de faire passer la nuit à un ou plusieurs enfants avec lesquels l'employé est en contact dans un contexte professionnel (bénéficiaires du programme) à leur domicile ou dans tout autre lieu de résidence ou d'hébergement personnel ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de discuter de leurs propres activités sexuelles, y compris de leurs rêves et de leurs fantasmes, ou de discuter avec des enfants de leur utilisation de matériel sexuellement orienté ou explicite, tel que de la pornographie, des vidéos ou du matériel sur ou à partir d'Internet ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de posséder, de regarder, de diffuser en continu, de télécharger d'Internet, etc. tout matériel à caractère sexuel (magazines, cartes, vidéos, films, vêtements, etc.) sur la propriété de l'organisation ou en présence d'enfants, sauf si cela est expressément autorisé dans le cadre d'un programme éducatif pré-autorisé ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'utiliser des ordinateurs, des téléphones portables, des caméras vidéo/numériques ou d'autres appareils électroniques pour accéder à, visualiser, créer, télécharger ou distribuer de la pornographie enfantine, y compris des images abusives d'enfants ou de jeunes ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'utiliser des ordinateurs, des téléphones portables,

des caméras vidéo et numériques, ou d'autres dispositifs ou supports électroniques pour exploiter, harceler ou intimider des enfants ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA de s'habiller, se déshabiller, prendre un bain ou une douche en présence d'enfants ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'exercer une discrimination, de faire preuve de différence ou de préférence à l'égard de certains enfants ou de les favoriser ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'utiliser les punitions physiques de quelque manière que ce soit pour gérer le comportement des enfants. Aucune forme de discipline physique n'est acceptable. Cette interdiction inclut les fessées, les gifles, les pincements, les coups ou toute autre force physique. La force physique ne peut être utilisée que pour mettre fin à un comportement susceptible de causer un préjudice immédiat à l'individu, à un enfant ou à d'autres personnes ; Il est interdit aux affiliés de la POA d'utiliser, de posséder, de distribuer ou d'être sous l'influence de l'alcool, de drogues illégales ou de l'abus de drogues légales lorsqu'ils participent ou aident à des programmes ou des activités destinés aux enfants ; Chaque programme pour enfants doit avoir un ratio établi de 2 adultes pour 1 à 20 enfants. Le respect du ratio établi est requis en tout temps, y compris pour les activités qui se déroulent à l'extérieur des locaux de l'organisme ; Lorsque des garçons, des filles et d'autres identités de genre participent, des adultes de sexe masculin, féminin et d'autres identités de genre doivent être présents ; Les conseils/réunions en tête-à-tête avec les enfants se dérouleront dans un lieu ouvert, public ou autre, où des conversations privées sont possibles mais se déroulent à la vue des autres ou en présence d'un autre adulte ; Lorsqu'ils supervisent ou assistent des activités privées, telles que l'habillage ou la douche des enfants, les affiliés de la POA restent dans une zone observable par d'autres adultes ou travaillent en

binôme ; Les parents ou les tuteurs doivent remplir des formulaires d'autorisation écrite avant que les sociétés affiliées à POA ne transportent les enfants pour une activité parrainée par la société ou à quelque fin que ce soit ; et Mettre en œuvre une approche de sauvegarde qui reconnaît et répond aux risques et aux besoins spécifiques de sauvegarde des différentes identités de genre et autres identités, y compris prendre des mesures appropriées pour lutter contre les préjugés sexistes et les autres formes de discrimination et de violence qui peuvent résulter de ces préjugés. Toutes les approches doivent tenir pleinement compte du genre en considérant les besoins spécifiques de sauvegarde des filles, des garçons et des autres identités de genre pour promouvoir l'égalité, l'équité et, en fin de compte, accroître la sécurité et la protection. Procédure de plainte pour la sauvegarde des enfants Les affiliés de la POA doivent signaler immédiatement toute violation de la politique de protection de l'enfance par l'un des moyens énumérés ci-dessous. Contactez le superviseur immédiat, le directeur du programme ou du pays du délinquant présumé. Contactez le service d'assistance téléphonique externe et confidentiel de POA, Foster Thomas, au +1 (443)-951-4951. Foster Thomas est une partie extérieure qui transmettra toute préoccupation au comité d'éthique du conseil d'administration. Les rapports peuvent être faits de manière anonyme. Contactez le service interne des RH de POA à l'adresse poahr@partners.net. Toutes les plaintes feront l'objet d'une enquête rapide et approfondie, et chaque plainte sera traitée de manière confidentielle. Les plaintes feront l'objet d'une enquête impartiale et si elles sont jugées véridiques, la POA prendra immédiatement des mesures correctives. Toute personne reconnue coupable d'avoir commis une EES fera l'objet de mesures disciplinaires appropriées pouvant aller jusqu'au licenciement. Représailles pour une plainte relative à l'EES La POA

interdit toute mesure de représailles à l'encontre de toute personne qui exerce légitimement ses droits en vertu de la présente politique, dépose une plainte ou participe/coopère à une enquête. Il ne doit pas y avoir d'ingérence, de coercition, de discrimination, de représailles ou de représailles à l'encontre d'une personne pour avoir déposé une plainte valide et véridique ou signalé une violation. Toute personne qui pense faire l'objet d'une discrimination pour avoir déposé une plainte ou participé à une enquête de manière légitime doit immédiatement déposer un rapport par l'un des moyens mentionnés ci-dessus. Toute personne qui a connaissance d'un éventuel harcèlement ou de représailles doit immédiatement déposer un rapport afin que l'organisation puisse enquêter en temps voulu et de manière confidentielle. La POA prendra des mesures contre toute personne, qu'elle fasse ou non l'objet d'une plainte, qui cherche à exercer ou exerce des représailles contre les plaignants, les témoins ou les victimes. Les employés peuvent faire l'objet de mesures disciplinaires, pouvant aller jusqu'au licenciement. Les autres personnes qui travaillent avec la POA peuvent voir leur relation avec la POA résiliée. Veuillez vous référer à la politique de protection de l'enfance pour de plus amples informations et conseils. J'ai lu et j'accepte cette politique. En signant ci-dessous, j'accepte de me conformer aux politiques susmentionnées. Je m'engage également à suivre l'une des formations/cours sur la protection de l'enfance énumérés dans la politique de protection de l'enfance dans les 30 jours suivant la signature du présent code de conduite, puis une fois par an. SignatureDate Nom (en caractères d'imprimerie)
