chunks.text: EMPLOYEE RECOGNITION AND AWARDS POLICY Effective XXXX Table of Contents Letter from President and CEO3 1.General Policy and Purpose4 2.Eligibility4 3.Types of Contribution Eligible for Recognition4 4.Method of Delivering the Reward4 5.On-the-Spot (OTS) Awards5 5.1Types of Contribution Eligible for Recognition for On-the-Spot Awards6 5.2Individual/Annual Dollar Limitation6 5.3Frequency6 5.4Process for On-the-Spot Award6 6.Formal Honor/Performance Awards7 6.1Process of Formal Program7 6.2Nomination Form7 6.3Frequency8 6.4Types of Contribution Eligible for Recognition for Formal Honor/Performance Awards8 6.4.1CEO/President’s Award8 CEO/President’s Award8 6.4.2New Employee Award9 New Employee Award9 6.4.3Outstanding Team Award9 Outstanding Team Award9 6.4.4Outstanding Team Award10 Outstanding Manager Award10 6.4.5Sepassi Spirit Award11 Sepassi Spirit Award11 6.4.6Exemplifies Partners Value Award12 Exemplifies Partners Value Award12 6.4.7Years of Service Recognition Award14 Years of Service Recognition14 6.4.8Employee of the Quarter Award14 Employee of the Quarter14 Letter from President and CEO Dear Colleagues, It gives me great pride to be at the helm of an organization as extraordinary as Partners. Our work makes such a huge difference in the lives of so many people because of the deep commitment and determination of Partners staff to fulfilling our mission across the Americas. I believe it is important to ensure that the extraordinary efforts of our employees continue to be recognized across Partners and beyond, and that we continue to highlight and recognize particularly exceptional accomplishments and achievements of individuals and teams each year. Partners is dedicated to connecting, serving, and changing lives across the Americas. I recognize that to do the work we do; our global team of extraordinarily talented and dedicated individuals work tirelessly every day to make a difference. The Partners Employee Recognition and Awards Program is intended to celebrate

that commitment and the outstanding contributions of our employees. I encourage you to participate by nominating your deserving colleagues. Details of the timeline, nomination process and a description of the awards are enclosed in the guide. I look forward to the continued success of our recognition and awards program, and to working with you to use it to foster a positive work environment. Best wishes, John McPhail President and CEOPartners of the Americas General Policy and Purpose This policy aims to recognize and reward performing employees throughout Partners of the Americas Inc. (Partners). We want to not just hire the best talent, but retain them too and therefore this policy will work towards appreciating and rewarding employees through their tenure with us. Partners recognizes employees for their contributions through a transparent and objective awards process. This process drives the acknowledgment of outstanding contributions by staff, innovation, creativity, teamwork, and years of service to Partners. Eligibility Recipients of awards must be active employees at the time of nomination and receipt of the award. Types of Contribution Eligible for Recognition The following are some different contributions of performance that a supervisor or Partners would like to recognize an employee or team under this policy/program. Length of service, generally in five-year increments. Exemplary one-time achievement Add quality to the work process or product Provide superior performance beyond job responsibilities Strong individual or team performance The ability to manage or champion change Innovation Systems improvements Donor retention Morale-building Talent acquisition and retention Market diversification Technological advances Significant personal development Actions that embody the Partners' core values. Method of Delivering the Reward Partners believe that recognition is not limited to monetary rewards, we happily appreciate our employee through innovative and

unique ways which are different based on the achievements. Non-monetary awards such as simple spoken or written thank you notes, or/and private or public recognition, team lunches, or time-off day, Monetary awards such as on-the-spot bonuses, gift, gifts cards. All monetary awards will be subject to taxes. Tiers Value $$ Value Hours Description Tier 1 $25-$200 0 hours An accomplishment that has aided in meeting unit goals; refer a new hire and remained employed after 90 days*, deserving of recognition because the employee exceeded expectations for his or her positions. Impact: limited to a unit *Referral program Officer-$25 Sr. Officer-$50 Associate Director-$100 Director-$150 VP-$200 Tier 2 $201-$299 4 hours An accomplishment that has substantially contributed to achieving unit objectives or resulted in a marked change in Partners’ policy or procedures. Impact whole unit Tier 3 $300-$399 8 hours An accomplishment that effects a total change in policies or procedures that cross unit and/or is exceptional in advancing multiple units’ objectives. Impact more than two units Tier 4 $400-$500 16 hours A rare level of achievement characterized by the initiation policies or procedures and substantial promotion/achievement of units or overall Partners strategic goals. Impact: cross-units, internally, or externally impact On-the-Spot (OTS) Awards To recognize employees for their day-to-day efforts that contribute in a special way to getting the job done. Under this award program, supervisors may give immediate recognition to recognize and reward employee excellence. An On-the-Spot award is granted by supervisors to recognize accomplishments that represent steps toward achievement of organizational goals or purposes, but for which higher-level recognition such as honor awards, performance awards, or superior accomplishment awards are not appropriate. An OTS should not be used in place of paying overtime or compensatory time and is not meant to replace other traditional

methods of recognition, but rather to increase supervisors' options in rewarding and reinforcing employee excellence. Rather than recognizing overall performance, the OTS acknowledges one or more specific instances of exemplary performance. Receipt of an OTS in this program does not preclude the same employee from receiving honorary recognition, a cash award, or an increase based on performance when criteria for such recognition are otherwise met. Types of Contribution Eligible for Recognition for On-the-Spot Awards The following are some examples of the types of contributions best suited for an On-The-Spot Award: Completion of a short-term project in less time than expected or where there were unusual difficulties to overcome. Planning a special event which is particularly successful because of the employee’s personal efforts. Handling an unusually heavy workload, such as when co-workers are absent or when vacant positions are not filled immediately. Development of new or revised procedures or other contributions toward improvement of office productivity. Completion of a significant special assignment that is outside of normal job responsibilities. Contributions that enable a unit to make more effective use of its resources. Contributions that improve the public awareness and/or understanding of Partners programs. Helping a co-worker who has an unusually heavy workload or a crash project. Refer a candidate who become an official staff and remain employed after 90 days. Voluntary participation in support of efforts related to the mission of Partners. Individual/Annual Dollar Limitation The dollar value of an individual may not exceed a total of $500 in a single calendar year, including any monetization received as part of a group or for on-the-spot awards. Individual supervisors are responsible for assuring compliance with the $500 limitation. Please note that any formal honor/performance awards are excluded from this amount. Frequency There is no limitation on the

number of OTS awards that an employee is eligible to receive if the dollar limitation is not exceeded. Process for On-the-Spot Award Step 1 The unit supervisor should decide that a contribution is worthy of recognition and determine the award tier Step 2 The unit supervisor prepares and signs the Recommendation for On-the-Spot Recognition Form. In Type and Amount of Recognition, select the dollar amount and award item. In addition, include the total amount year-to-date. The current award amount being recommended is not included in that total. Note: An employee, either as an individual or as part of a group, may not receive awards under this program which exceed a total of $500 in a single calendar year. Step 3 Submit a form for each employee nominated to Unit VP. Then forward the request to the HR, CFO, and then for final approval to CEO/President. Step 4 Upon approval, HR should notify the supervisor that the request has been approved and the employee is the recipient of an OTS award. At that time, inform the employee that he/she may redeem the gift certificate or be inform of when to expect the OTS to reflect on their paycheck. Formal Honor/Performance Awards Performance Award have been outlined to recognize our hard-working employees per category. CEO/President’s Award New Employee Award Outstanding Team Award Outstanding Manager Award Sepassi Spirit Award Exemplifies Partners Values Award Years of Service Recognition Employee of the Quarter Process of Formal Program Month Activities TBD CEO/President announces Partners event to be held TBD Call for Award nominations. TBD Nominations process closed. TBD Vice Presidents confirm employee representative for their department to participate on selection committee. TBD HR reviews nominations to ensure criteria eligibility and sends the final award nomination SharePoint library to each unit Vice President to approve nominations for their department. TBD VP’s return nomination binders to HR. TBD HR forwards revised

binders to selection committee members. TBD VP of HR facilitates meeting with selection committee to determine nomination recommendations for CEO consideration. TBD The President/CEO selects and approves final recipients. TBD Awards preparation is completed by HR. TBD Final recipients are recognized at an appropriate event. Nomination Form All global employees – US-based, expatriate, third country national and host country national staff are eligible to submit nominations or to be nominated in their respective categories. If field offices have selected employees through their own recognition program, consider submitting those names as nominations. Many employees may only feel comfortable nominating their peers in their native language and will be accepted. Home Office HR will translate the document. Staff unable to directly access the SharePoint Nomination Form should submit their nomination via email using the Nomination Form Email Template. Frequency Once announced by the CEO/President, the formal awards will be celebrated on annual basis at a Partners event. Types of Contribution Eligible for Recognition for Formal Honor/Performance Awards CEO/President’s Award CEO/President’s Award Description: This award recognizes one staff member who has made an exceptional contribution to Partners. The award is conferred on an individual who is recognized as an example of the kind of performance to be emulated by all. The awardee should be someone who has consistently demonstrated extraordinary dedication to the work and resulting in some clear and tangible benefit to Partners. Criteria Achievements that clearly and tangibly benefit the programs and/or services of Partners Recognition by peers and supervisors for their level of commitment and excellent performance results. Exceptional contributions to the work of Partners beyond normal expectations. Exemplifies the highest levels of integrity and adherence to the values, principles, policies, and procedures of Partners.

Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. He/She is inSPIRES. Nominator Members of the Senior Management Team in conjunction with the nominees’ supervisor. Eligibility All Partners staff members. Recognition Desk Globe (TBD) Guidelines Nominations submitted by members of the Senior Management Team. Committee: N/A Final Approval CEO/President New Employee Award New Employee Award Description: This award is granted in recognition of outstanding contributions by a staff member with two years of service or less whose performance has been exceptional. The individual has made substantial contributions to the achievement of Partners’ mission. Criteria Demonstrated strong commitment and significant contributions toward the mission and objectives of Partners Demonstrated initiative, resourcefulness and/or creativity. Accepts new challenges and views them as career development opportunities. Demonstrates a results-oriented focus. Maintains a positive attitude. Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. He/She is inSPIRES Nominator Nominators can include direct reports and co-workers but will require the approval of the Nominee’s supervisor. A supervisor of the nominee can also nominate. Eligibility Staff members with 2 years of service or less at the time of nomination. Recognition Clock Guidelines Limited to two employees: one from HQ and one from the field. Committee: Nominations approved by respective unit VP then forwarded by the unit VP to Selection Committee for final selection. Final Approval CEO/President Outstanding Team Award Outstanding Team Award Description: This award is granted in recognition of outstanding contributions by a functional team or an inter-programmatic or ad hoc team whose performance has been exceptional and has made substantial contributions to the achievement of Partners’ Mission. Criteria Demonstrated strong commitment and significant contributions toward the mission and objectives of Partners. Observable

contribution to the improvement and/or introduction of methods and procedures required for the execution of programs. Evidence of teamwork, dedication to duty and esprit de corps, by using the capabilities and strengths of all the team members to achieve the particular result that is being recognized by the award. Demonstrated specific and substantial contribution to meeting the needs of external or internal clients of Partners (counterparts or other Partners units). The team/project must have achieved a specific goal or result during the reporting period and at the same time maintained a supportive and healthy work environment for all involved. Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. The team is inSPIRES Nominator Nominators must be thoroughly familiar with the work of the teams being nominated and must be current staff members of Partners Eligibility All Partners staff members. Recognition Certificate of Appreciation signed by the CEO/President for each team member Guidelines This award will be limited to two teams. Teams can consist of the following structure: support, functional, and/or inter-programmatic teams, departmental teams, and cross departmental teams. Two teams will be predominantly from the field, and two teams will be predominantly from HQ. Committee: Nominations approved by respective unit VP then forwarded by the unit VP to selection committee for final selection. se Final Approval CEO/President Outstanding Team Award Outstanding Manager Award Description: This award recognizes outstanding performance by Partners staff members responsible for leading program or administrative areas and teams. An outstanding manager not only produces excellent results, but also consistently demonstrates good management practices and maintains a positive work environment for the entire team under his or her leadership. Criteria Demonstration of efficient and effective management of human resources and leadership by displaying qualities such as:

Being a role model for staff Sensitivity to cultural diversity Acknowledging the contributions of others Providing continuous constructive feedback Building an environment of tolerance and trust Promoting teamwork within his or her area of responsibility and in any other specifically assigned ad hoc project Promoting the establishment of networks to increase inter-programmatic collaboration with other areas of Partners Building understanding and forging partnerships with others in and outside the Partners Coaching and mentoring staff under his or her supervision Design of administrative policies and procedures resulting in significant progress toward efficiency and effectiveness and/or achieving significant savings related to a particular program or to Partners as a whole. Evidence of having managed effectively and efficiently that part of Partners’ programmatic or managerial area under his or her responsibility, as reported by peers, supervisor, donors, etc. Sets an example of integrity and adherence to the values, principles, policies, and procedures of Partners. Promotes the dissemination and exchange of the knowledge within the team and throughout Partners. Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. He/She is inSPIRES Nominator Nominators must be thoroughly familiar with the work of the person being nominated. Nominators can include direct reports and co-workers but will require the approval of the nominee’s supervisor. A supervisor of the nominee can also nominate. Eligibility Any staff member in a managerial position. Recognition Clock Guidelines Limited to two employees per year. Committee: Nominations approved by respective unit VP then forwarded by the unit VP to Selection Committee for final selection. Final Approval CEO/President Sepassi Spirit Award Sepassi Spirit Award Description: This award will honor those who inspire and empower every generation in the spirit of service and partnership. This award is granted in recognition of

outstanding contributions by a staff member not in a management role and whose performance has been exceptional and has made substantial contributions to the achievement of Partners’ Mission. Criteria Demonstrated strong commitment and significant contributions toward the mission and objectives of Partners. Demonstrated initiative, resourcefulness and/or creativity. Accepts new challenges and views them as career development opportunities. Demonstrates a results-oriented focus. Maintains a positive attitude. Demonstrated specific and substantial contribution to meeting the needs of external or internal clients of Partners (counterparts, donors, beneficiaries, etc.). Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. He/She is inSPIRES. Nominator Nominators can include direct reports and co-workers but will require the approval of the nominee’s supervisor. A supervisor of the nominee can also nominate. Eligibility Any staff member not in a management role. Recognition Clock Guidelines Limited to two employees per year. Committee: Nominations approved by respective unit VP then forwarded by the unit VP to Selection Committee for final selection. Final Approval CEO/President Exemplifies Partners Value Award Exemplifies Partners Value Award Description: This award will be given to an employee who has exhibited the 7 Partners: inSPIRES in their work. Our Values are 7 commitments that we use to define our work, our approach and who we are. We use these to examine ourselves and to ensure that we remain focused. This Criteria Service We serve individuals and groups by empowering them with the tools and resources to learn, grow, and serve their own local communities. Partnership We are committed to mutually beneficial relationships that are multicultural, diverse, and built on empathy, respect, and collaboration. Impact We strive to maintain long-term results through lasting commitments, deep-rooted local presence, and evidence-based projects that focus on

systems-change. Resilience We confront societal challenges with strength and adaptability by building the capacity of people, families, and communities to actively respond to adversity. Participates in at least three professional development opportunities to increase technical skills or behavioral competencies. Impact We strive to maintain long-term results through lasting commitments, deep-rooted local presence, and evidence-based projects that focus on systems-change. Impact We strive to maintain long-term results through lasting commitments, deep-rooted local presence, and evidence-based projects that focus on systems-change. Respect We cultivate an environment of respect through a shared leadership that embraces mutual understanding and compassion across inclusive and diverse communities. Empowerment We trust in community-led solutions and encourage and inspire citizens to become agents of change. Sustainability We recognize the increasing risks posed by human and environmental degradation, especially to already vulnerable communities, and strive to implement programs with a focus on long-term impact, environmental protection, and climate change preparedness. Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. He/She is inSPIRES Nominator Nominators must be thoroughly familiar with the work of the person being nominated. Nominators can include direct reports and co-workers but will require the approval of the Nominee’s supervisor. A supervisor of the nominee can also nominate. Eligibility All Partners staff members. Recognition Clock Guidelines Limited to two employees: one from HO and one from the field. Committee: Nominations approved by respective unit VP then forwarded by the unit VP to Selection Committee for final selection. Final Approval CEO/President Years of Service Recognition Award Years of Service Recognition Description: The purpose of this category is to acknowledge staff members who have served Partners for an extended period of time.

Criteria Staff members will be recognized for having completed the number of years of service as employees of Partners. Nominator N/A Eligibility Staff members who are assigned to an official position at HO, Field Offices, or who work remotely. Contingent on the number of years that the staff member has completed by the end of each quarter. Employees will be recognized in increments of 5 years of service (e.g., 5, 10, 15, 20, etc. years). Recognition Service Anniversary Certificate reflective of the number of years recognized. These certificates will be given to the staff member by their respective unit Vice President at the close of each quarter. 5 years to 15 years – Partners Mug 20 years - Engraved Pen 25 years - Marble Clock Guidelines Only time of service accumulated during direct employment is counted. Time worked as a consultant is not included in the calculation of years of service. Final Approval HR Employee of the Quarter Award Employee of the Quarter Description: This award will honor those who recognize employees who have served at Partners in an exceptional manner by exemplifying outstanding quality service through his or her work and exhibiting a positive and supportive attitude. Criteria Serves as a role model to others Dedicated to fulfilling job responsibilities Goes above and beyond the requirements of the job Has a team player attitude High overall quality of performance Knowledgeable of Partners’ policy and procedures Takes initiatives Requires little supervision Controls high stress situations tactfully and calmly Epitomizes our core values within the daily work environment. He/She is inSPIRES. Nominator Nominators can include direct reports and co-workers but will require the approval of the nominee’s supervisor. A supervisor of the nominee can also nominate. Eligibility Any staff member not in a management role. Recognition Plaque and $$ Guidelines Limited to one per quarter Committee: Nominations approved by respective supervisor are
forwarded to unit VP to present at the SLT meeting. Final Approval Majority Vote

chunks.text: Executive Leadership Team Compensation Policy Effective April 1, 2026 John McPhail President & CEO Table of Contents 1.Purpose3 2.Applications3 3.Approval Process3 4.ELT Compensation Structure3 4.1Base Salary3 4.2Performance-Based Incentive Compensation4 5.Performance-Based Incentive Structure4 5.1Annual Incentive Pool and Cap4 5.2Review Process for Incentive Payments4 5.3Payment Frequency5 6.Policy Administration and Review5 Purpose The goal of this policy is to establish a compensation framework for the Executive Leadership Team (ELT) of Partners of the Americas (Partners), defined as Grade F in the organization’s salary scale, that provides a structured and competitive approach to total compensation. This framework is designed to encourage high performance through incentive-based rewards tied to organizational goals and targets, while remaining compliant with applicable federal regulations, including 2 CFR 200. Applications This policy applies to employees classified as Grade F within Partners’ salary scale, defined as members of the ELT and applies only to U.S.-based staff who are employed and paid through Partners’ U.S. payroll. This policy does not apply to the President & CEO, whose compensation and benefits are determined and approved by the Board of Directors. Approval Process ELT offer letters and compensation packages, including salary and benefits, must be reviewed and approved by the President & CEO. The President & CEO is also responsible for reviewing and evaluating the performance of ELT, including approval of any performance-based compensation incentives. ELT Compensation Structure The compensation structure for ELT employees is designed to provide a competitive and market-aligned total compensation package that supports the recruitment and retention of highly qualified leadership. This structure balances fixed and performance-based components to incentivize the achievement of organizational goals and reinforce accountability for results. All

compensation is established and administered in a manner that is reasonable, consistently applied across similarly situated roles. Base Salary Base salary for ELT employees shall not exceed the GS-15 Step 10 equivalent. The applicable salary cap will be reviewed and updated annually in accordance with the calendar year to reflect any changes to the U.S. Government General Schedule pay scale. For 2026, the GS-15 Step 10 salary cap is $164,301 USD annually. Base salary will be allocated across programs and departments in proportion to the employee’s level of effort (LOE), as reflected in approved timesheets. Performance-Based Incentive Compensation Partners may provide Performance-Based Incentive Compensation (PBIC) as part of total compensation. PBIC is considered a fringe benefit and must be administered in accordance with applicable federal regulations, including 2 CFR 200. PBIC is designed to reward performance and is not guaranteed compensation. It must be earned based on measurable performance. Any PBIC will be allocated across programs and departments in proportion to the employee’s average LOE over the applicable period. PBIC is considered eligible compensation for purposes of retirement benefits. Accordingly, PBIC will be included in the calculation of employee and employer contributions to the organization’s 403(b) retirement plan, including any applicable employer match. For purposes of calculating vacation payout and severance (if applicable), the employee’s average hourly or monthly rate will be based on the average compensation earned during the current calendar year. This calculation will include both base salary and PBIC earned, ensuring that total compensation is reflected in determining applicable payouts. Performance-Based Incentive Structure The PBIC is established as a variable component of total compensation and is intended to reward contributions to programmatic success, financial performance, and organizational leadership. Incentives are not

guaranteed and must be earned based on predefined performance criteria that are documented in advance and applied consistently across ELT roles. Annual Incentive Pool and Cap Each ELT member can draw funds from a PBIC pool, subject to a maximum annual cap of $35,795 USD. This cap represents the maximum potential incentive compensation that may be earned within a calendar year. The PBIC cap will be reviewed annually on January 1st and may be adjusted based on organizational priorities, market conditions, and applicable regulatory requirements. Review Process for Incentive Payments PBIC will be reviewed on a quarterly basis to assess progress against established performance goals and metrics. These reviews will determine the incentive payments. ELT members will earn PBIC payment for achieving the following: Lead Organizational Advancement unit and oversee successful submission of one (1) bid valued over $500,000 USD or more. Payment of up to 10% of the annual PBIC cap.* Submit one (1) quality and complete detailed programmatic/progress narrative to the donor, either annually, semi-annually, or quarterly, as defined by the donor award. Payment of up to 3% of the annual PBIC cap.* *Once the maximum PBIC pool cap has been reached as defined in section 5.1, no further payments will be made for the achievements in the calendar year. The cap resets on January 1st of each year. Payment Frequency PBIC payments will be paid on a quarterly basis, following the review and evaluation of performance metrics. Payments will reflect the level of achievement during the applicable period. Policy Administration and Review This policy is administered by the Human Resources and Finance departments, which are responsible for ensuring consistent application, proper documentation, and compliance. This policy will be reviewed annually, or as needed, to reflect changes in organizational priorities, market conditions, and regulatory requirements. Any updates or revisions must be approved by the
President & CEO.

chunks.heading: ALLOWANCES
chunks.text: Air travel to duty post: Covered by [POA or prior] employer/ POA will cover [difference or total] from cost-construct if any. Visa and immigration assistance: Supporting the application for a work visa for the employee. Partners will support the application of a residency visa only for the ex-pat’s immediate family (for this matter, the term “immediate family” relates to the spouse and dependents of the ex-pat). Excess baggage: Up to 50 pounds of accompanied personal baggage per traveler in addition to that regularly allowed with economy ticket, provided that the total number of pounds does not exceed what is regularly allowed for first class travelers. Reimbursable upon receipt. Relocation costs - household goods shipment to post: $XX,000 in relocation costs which are intended to cover but not limited to the following (as applicable): flights, movement of household goods, airfreight, shipment for land/sea container, and customs and taxes. Health & dental insurance: Coverage for health and dental will be a XXXX (indicate whether local or international) plan. 100% of the premium will be covered for the employee. 80% of spouse and all eligible dependents will be covered. Home of record leave: 10 days Home Leave time, pending eligibility in accordance with Ex-pat benefit policy. Home of record trip (airfare & transportation cost): To include round-trip economy airfare for employee and accompanying dependents to Home of Record. Partners will also cover any other transportation costs as necessary for the ex-pat to return to the home country including but not limited to taxis, buses, trains, etc. Rest & Relaxation (R&R): 7 days R&R Leave, pending eligibility in accordance with Ex-pat benefit policy. Each post eligible for R&R travel has a designated relief point. A list of eligible posts and their designated relief points is located in the Foreign Affairs Manual (FAM) 3 FAH-1 Exhibit H-3722. Alternatively, employees may select to use R&R in the U.S. or any U.S. territory.

Cost of living allowance (COLA): Applicable in accordance with US Dept of State rate for duty location. Currently calculated at XX% of gross salary. Subject to change. Living quarters/accommodation allowance: Up to a maximum of USD $XXX USD per month. Temporary quarters subsistence allowance: Up to 90 days (3 months) temporary accommodation up to a maximum of $XXX USD per month upon arrival while searching for long term housing, reimbursable upon receipts. Education allowance: Primary and secondary education for XX dependents at the maximum US Dept of State amount allowable for XXX location. Holidays XXX number of holidays, can select host country holidays, U.S., or combination of both. Demobilization costs - Repatriation travel to home of record: Round trip economy air travel for the employee and accompanying dependents by most direct route. Note 1: If the employee resigns within 24 months, he/she will not be eligible for repatriation benefits. Additionally, employees who choose to end employment with Partners within the first 24 months of assignment agree to reimburse Partners for prepaid housing and pre-paid education costs on a prorated basis equal to each month following the termination date. Note 2: Household goods furnishings include durable goods used to furnish and equip the employee’s

chunks.text:
Fitness Center Waiver and Release of Liability
In consideration of my use of the exercise equipment, gym, shower, and facilities provided by Partners of the Americas Inc. (Partners) whether in the hotel and/or at the office, I expressly agree and contract, on behalf of myself, my heirs, executors, administrators, successors and assigns, that Partners and its insurers, employees, officers, directors, and associates, shall not be liable for any damages arising from personal injuries (including death) sustained by me, or my guest in, on, or about the premises, or as a result of the use of the equipment or facilities, regardless of whether such injuries result, in whole or in part, from the negligence of Partners.
By the execution of this agreement, I accept and assume full responsibility for any and all injuries, damages (both economic and non-economic), and losses of any type, which may occur to me or my guest, and I hereby fully and forever release and discharge Partners, its insurers, employees, officers, directors, and associates, from any and all claims, demands, damages, rights of action, or causes of action, present or future, whether the same be known or unknown, anticipated, or unanticipated, resulting from or arising out the use of said equipment and facilities.
I expressly agree to indemnify and hold Partners harmless against any and all claims, demands, damages, rights of action, or causes of action, of any person or entity, that may arise from injuries or damages sustained by me or my guest.
I agree to be solely responsible for safety and wellbeing of my guest and myself. I understand that Partners does not provide supervision, instruction, or assistance for the use of the facilities and equipment.
I agree to comply with all rules imposed by Partners regarding the use of the facilities and equipment. I agree to conduct myself in a controlled and reasonable manner at all times, and to refrain from using any equipment in a manner inconsistent with its intended design and purpose.
I understand and acknowledge that the use of exercise equipment involves risk of serious injury, including permanent disability and death.
I understand and agree that Partners is not responsible for property that is lost, stolen, or damaged while in, on, or about the premises.
I understand and agree that my use of the facilities and equipment is only to be undertaken on my own personal time, and that my use of the facilities and equipment is not within the course or scope of my employment.
I HAVE READ THE FOREGOING WAIVER AND RELEASE OF LIABILITY AND VOLUNTARILY EXECUTED THIS DOCUMENT WITH FULL KNOWLEDGE OF ITS CONTENT.
Date:_____________________________ 
Signature: _________________________
Print Name: ________________________

documents.folder: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Harassment Policy

chunks.text:
Productive Work Environment/Sexual Harassment 
Partners intends to provide for its employees, and non-employees (such as interns, volunteers, consultants and independent contractors), a work environment that is pleasant, healthful, comfortable, and free from intimidation, hostility or other offenses which might interfere with their performance. Harassment of any sort – verbal, physical, environmental – is prohibited by this policy and will not be tolerated. Moreover, Partners will not tolerate harassment by any employee, non-employee or member of the Partners network. 
Partners does not tolerate discrimination on the basis of sex, race, color, creed, age, religion, disability, national origin, or any other legally prohibited characteristic. Harassment based on sex or any other legally prohibited basis is illegal and is contrary to the policy of Partners. 
Partners will not tolerate verbal or physical conduct which harasses, disrupts or interferes with work performance or which creates an intimidating, offensive or hostile work environment. In order to avoid circumstances where harassment becomes severe or pervasive, employees and non-employees should report all incidents of harassment immediately. While all forms of harassment are prohibited, Partners emphasizes that sexual harassment is specifically prohibited. Each employee and non-employee has the responsibility to ensure that the workplace is free of any form of sexual harassment. No Partners employees, non-employees or members of the Partners network shall threaten or insinuate, either explicitly or implicitly, that any person’s refusal to submit to sexual advances will adversely affect the work.
Other sexually harassing conduct in the workplace is also prohibited. Such conduct includes, but is not limited to: a) unwelcome sexual flirtations, touching, advances, or propositions; b) verbal abuse of a sexual nature; c) graphic or sexually suggestive comments; d) sexually degrading words to describe an individual; and e) the display, storage or transmission of sexually suggestive objects or pictures. 
Harassment Complaint Procedure 
If you believe you have been subjected to prohibited harassment or you are aware of any such conduct, the incident must be reported immediately, preferably in writing, to any supervisor or Senior Manager of Partners of the Americas. All complaints will be promptly and thoroughly investigated and each complaint will be handled as confidentially as possible under the circumstances. Partners prohibits retaliation against any person who files a harassment complaint or cooperates with an investigation. Complaints will be investigated impartially and if found to be truthful, Partners will take immediate corrective action. Anyone found to have engaged in harassment will be subject to appropriate disciplinary action up to, and including termination. 
Retaliation for Harassment Complaint 
Partners prohibits any retaliation against anyone who legitimately exercise their rights under this policy or who participate in an investigation. There is to be no interference, coercion, discrimination, reprisal or retaliation against any person for making a valid and truthful complaint or report of a violation. Any employees or non-employees who believe they are being discriminated against as a result of having legitimately filed a complaint or having truthfully participated in an investigation should immediately notify a member of management. Anyone who becomes aware of possible harassment or retaliation must immediately advise Partners of the Americas so it can be investigated in a timely and confidential manner.
This policy applies to all individuals when interacting with members of the Partners staff or network.
I have read and agree to this policy.
SignatureDate
Name (Please Print)

documents.path: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Interviewing Guidelines.docx
documents.folder: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual

chunks.text: Interviewing Guidelines Preparing for the Interview: Make sure you have read all paperwork on the applicant. Review the current position requirements. Select interview questions that will help determine the information you need in order to make a decision. Setting the Tone: Help make the applicant feel at ease – the more comfortable the applicant feels the more they will reveal. Make the applicant aware of what you do and how your position relates to the position for which he/she is applying. Let the applicant know about the organization (e.g., values, mission statement and organizational structure). Documenting the Interview: It is a good idea to take brief notes during the interview. Let the applicant know from the start that you will be taking notes. Refrain from writing comments on his/her application or resume. Take notes on a separate piece of paper and/or utilize the Interview Comments Form. Avoid subjective characterizations of the applicant that may be misinterpreted as discriminatory or irrelevant to the interviewing process. Concluding the Interview: Do not make any promises of employment. Verbal agreements often act as binding contracts. Avoiding Discrimination: When an applicant is interviewed, questions should focus on the applicant’s attitude, overall competence, motivation and general suitability for hire in a particular position. Avoid questions pertaining to or that may prompt a response about race, age, religion, marital status or non-job-related disabilities. General rule to remember: All questions should be specifically related to the essential functions of the job. If the question is not going to solicit pertinent information used to hire the person, then don’t ask. Even if the applicant volunteers this information, it should not be documented and interviewers should not ask or document any follow up or probing questions. Further, these aspects should not be used as a basis in the hiring decision. The following chart is a reference of legal and

discriminatory interview questions: Topic Legal Questions Discriminatory Questions Name What is your name? Have you ever used another name? Is any additional information relative to change of name, use of an assumed name, or nickname necessary to enable a check on your work and educational record? If yes, please explain. What is your maiden name? Age Statements that hire is subject to verification that applicant meets legal age requirements. If hired, can you offer proof of age? Are you over 18 years old? If under 18, can you, after employment, submit a work permit? How old are you? What is your birth date? Questions regarding dates of attendance of elementary or high school. Questions which tend to identify applicants over age 40. Sex, Marital Status, Family Status Do you have any responsibilities that conflict with the job attendance or travel requirements? Name and address of parent or guardian if applicant is a minor. Statement of ABC Company policy regarding work assignment of employees who are related. Are you married? What is your spouse’s name? What is your maiden name? Do you have children? Are you pregnant? What are your child care provisions? With whom do you reside? Do you live with your parents? Race None What is your race? Questions regarding applicant’s complexion or color of skin, eyes, hair. Birthplace or Citizenship Can you, after employment, submit verification of your legal right to work in the U.S.? Statement that such proof may be required after a decision is made to hire the candidate. Are you a U.S. citizen? Where were you (or relatives) born? Requirements that applicant produce naturalization, first papers or alien card prior to decision to hire. National Origin Languages applicant reads, speaks, or writes, if use of a language other than English is relevant to the job for which applicant is applying. Questions as to nationality, lineage, ancestry, national origin, descent , or percentage of applicant, applicant’s parents or spouse. What is

your mother tongue? What language do you commonly use? How applicant acquired ability to read, write or speak foreign language. Residence Place of residence Do you own or rent a home? Sex None Are you male or female? Credit Reporting None Any report which would indicate information which is otherwise illegal to ask, e.g., marital status, age, residency, etc. Physical description, Photograph Statement that photograph may be required after employment. Questions as to applicant’s height and weight. Require applicant to affix a photograph to application. Request applicant, as his or her option, to submit a photograph. Require a photograph after interview but before employment. Videotaping interviews. Interviewing Applicants with Disabilities: The Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), as amended by the ADA Amendments Act of 2008 (ADAAA), prohibits employers from discriminating against qualified applicants or employees with disabilities. Employers with 15 or more employees are covered. The ADA protects qualified individuals from discrimination on the basis of disability in all aspects of the employment process, including recruitment, hiring, rates of pay, upgrading and selection for training. The Act also requires a covered employer to reasonably accommodate a qualified individual unless it can show that by doing so it would suffer an undue hardship. There are strategies for determining the need for reasonable accommodation and information on an employer’s obligations under the law. The ADA also protects applicants and employees from discrimination based on their association with people with disabilities. Under the ADA/ADAAA, a “disability” is defined as a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more of the individual’s major life activities. The definition of a disability also includes having a record of such impairment or being regarded as having such an impairment (42 USC 12102(2)(B) ). Under the ADA/ADAAA, “major life activities” include caring

for oneself, performing manual tasks, seeing, hearing, eating, sleeping, walking, standing, lifting, bending, speaking, breathing, learning, reading, concentrating, thinking, communicating and working. In addition, major life activity also includes the operation of a major bodily systems, including the digestive, neurological, respiratory, circulatory, endocrine, reproductive and immune systems; the functioning of the bowels, bladder and brain; and normal cell growth (42 USC 12102(2)). Multiple impairments that combine to substantially limit a major life activity may also be considered a form of disability (29 CFR 1630.2(j)). A physical or mental impairment is defined as any physiological disorder or condition, cosmetic disfigurement, or anatomical loss affecting any of several body systems, including neurological and musculoskeletal systems (29 CFR 1630.2(h)(1)). Under the ADA/ADAAA, an impairment that is episodic or in remission constitutes a disability if it would substantially limit a major life activity when active (42 USC 12102(4)(C)). Physical characteristics such as left-handedness and personality traits such as being irresponsible or having poor judgment are not impairments covered under the ADA. A physical or mental impairment does not include impairments that are transitory and minor. “Transitory” is defined as an actual or expected duration of 6 months or less. Under the ADA/ADAA, the determination of whether an impairment substantially limits a major life activity must be made without regard to mitigating measures that relieve or improve the impairment (42 USC 12102(4)(E)). Examples of mitigating measures listed in the ADAAA include: medication; medical supplies, equipment or appliances; low-vision devices; prosthetics, including limbs and devices; hearing aide, cochlear implants or other implantable hearing devices; mobility devices; oxygen therapy equipment and supplies; use of assistive technology; reasonable accommodations or auxiliary aids or

services; and learned behavioral or adaptive neurological modifications. Ordinary eyeglasses or contact lenses that are intended to fully correct vision are not considered mitigating measures and may be considered in determining whether an impairment is a disability. A person who satisfies the prerequisites for the position (i.e., the appropriate education, employment experience or licenses) and can perform the essential functions of the job is considered a qualified individual. The determination of whether an individual with a disability is qualified should be based on the individual’s capabilities at the time of the employment decision and not on speculation that the disability may worsen in the future or may cause increased health insurance premiums or workers’ compensation costs. A record of a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits a major life activity generally includes two groups of people: those who have a history of such an impairment and those who have been misclassified as having such an impairment. Individuals who have recovered from mental or emotional illness, heart disease or cancer are common examples of the first group; those who have been misclassified as having mental illness are a common example of the second group. There are many types of records that potentially contain this information (e.g., education, medical or employment records). Under the ADA/ADAAA, an individual is “regarded as” having a disability if an individual establishes that he or she was subjected to an action prohibited under the ADA because of an actual or perceived impairment whether or not the impairment actually limits or is perceived to limit a major life activity. You must provide a reasonable accommodation if needed to enable an applicant to have an equal opportunity in the interview process. You may place in your advertisements a request that an individual who needs an accommodation for an interview should request it in advance. Such accommodations can

include: An accessible location for persons with mobility disabilities; A sign interpreter for a person who is deaf; Written responses rather than oral responses for a person with a voice or hearing disability; Written material in large print or Braille for a person with a visual disability; A reader for a person who is blind; More time for a person who has a speech disability or a mental disability that causes slow speech or a short attention span; or Simpler language for a person with a mental disability. With regards to pre-employment tests, generally you may not conduct a medical examination of an employee until after you have made a conditional job offer. In other words, before making a job offer you cannot require an employee to undergo a medical examination. However, you can condition a job offer on successful completion of a medical examination. You can offer a job to an applicant based on the condition that the applicant successfully passes a medical examination. At the time of interview, you can: Ask an applicant if he or she is able to perform the essential functions of job; Ask how the individual would perform the task and with what accommodation; or Administer tests which measure skills and abilities needed to do a job. For example, a typing test, sales demonstration test, physical skills test, math test, spelling test and the like. Again, the test must be designed for the purpose of testing an essential job function. You may be required to accommodate persons with disabilities when they take a test. Examples of accommodation for testing include: Substituting a written test for an oral test for persons with impaired speaking or hearing skills; Administering a test in large print; Administering a test in Braille; Allowing persons with limited use of their hands to record test answers by tape recorder, dictation to an individual or a computer; Providing extra time to complete a test for persons with dyslexia; Simplifying test language for a person who has

limited language skills because of a head injury; Scheduling rest breaks for a person with a mental disability that requires such relief; or Assuring that a test site is accessible for a person with a mobility disability. Also, please keep in mind that with the Civil Rights Act of 1991, test scores cannot be adjusted based on a person’s age, race, color, gender, national origin, religion, and disability and the like. Tests for illegal use of drugs are not medical examinations under the ADA and are not subject to the restrictions on such medical examinations. Keep in mind that many states do regulate drug testing. Further, current drug abusers or individuals whose current use of alcohol interferes with their job performance are not protected by the ADA. In contrast, recovering alcoholics and recovering drug abusers are protected. Not only may the employer not ask the employee directly about disabilities, but neither can it obtain such information from other sources. For example, before making a conditional offer of employment, an employer may not ask previous employers or other sources about an applicant’s: Disabilities; Illnesses; Workers’ compensation history; Absences due to illness; Health care claims; Physical disabilities; or Mental disabilities. You can ask a previous employer about: Dates of employment; Job functions and task performed by the applicant; The quality of work; the quantity of work; How job functions were performed; Attendance records; or If an applicant has a known disability and has indicated that he or she could perform a function with a reasonable accommodation, you may ask a previous employer about the accommodations made by it. You may reject an applicant because the disability poses a direct threat to health and safety. This means you must: Be able to show a significant, current risk of substantial harm; Identify the specific risk; Document the risk by objective medical or other factual evidence regarding the particular individual; and
Establish that you cannot eliminate or reduce the direct threat by a reasonable accommodation.

documents.path: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Non-Discrimination Policy/Non-Discrimination Policy - English.docx
documents.folder: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Non-Discrimination Policy

chunks.text:
Non-Discrimination/Affirmative Action
Partners of the Americas is an equal opportunity employer. Partners does not discriminate regardless of actual or perceived race, creed, color, sex (including pregnancy, childbirth and other pregnancy-related conditions), religion, national origin, citizenship, sexual orientation, personal appearance, gender identity or expression, physical or mental handicap or disability, age (18 or over), marital status (including parenthood), family responsibilities, matriculation, genetic information, political affiliation, veteran status or any other areas of discrimination prohibited by applicable law. This policy applies to all terms, conditions, and privileges of employment including hiring, training and staff development, promotion and compensation.
Partners promotes diversity and equal opportunity in the workplace in accordance with all applicable Federal and local law requirements.
Employees with questions or concerns about any type of discrimination in the workplace are encouraged to contact their supervisor. If you believe that you have been the victim of any type of discrimination or are aware of such conduct, you must promptly notify your supervisor or a Senior Manager of Partners. Employees may raise concerns and make reports without fear of reprisal.
Anyone found to be engaging in any type of unlawful discrimination will be subject to disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment.
Persons with Disabilities
As part of its commitment to a diverse workforce, Partners does not discriminate in its hiring or employment practices against qualified individuals with disabilities who can perform the essential job functions with or without reasonable accommodation. In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, Partners adheres to the following guidelines:
The application process is used solely to determine whether a candidate's qualifications and abilities reasonably match job requirements. Therefore, applicants are not queried about their physical condition or medical history (except where it appears that essential job functions could not be performed without accommodations, in which case only appropriate inquiries relating to functional limitations are made).
Partners will make reasonable accommodations to enable a qualified individual with a disability to perform essential job functions, unless doing so will create an undue hardship.
Employees who need a reasonable accommodation should inform their supervisor or a Senior Manager of Partners as soon as possible and Partners will engage in an interactive process with the employee to evaluate potential accommodations.
By signing below, I certify that I have read and understood the statement written above. Signature: Date: 
Printed Name:

documents.folder: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Overtime Policy

chunks.text: OVERTIME POLICY Objective Partners’ overtime policy is applicable to those employees who have an FLSA (Fair Labor Standards Act) designation of non-exempt indicated on their offer letter or memo to file. It is also indicated on the job description. All employees will need to adhere to the pertinent clause below based on their exemption: Exempt Employees: Exempt employees are not eligible for overtime under any circumstances. Non-Exempt Employees: Non-exempt employees while eligible per law to charge overtime may not charge any overtime per Partners’ policy unless pre-approved in writing from their supervisor and if applicable their grants officer. The compensation for overtime is one-and-one half (1½) times the employee’s normal hourly rate for all time worked over 40 hours in any particular work week. Partners’ work week is seven (7) consecutive days, beginning on Monday at 12:00 am and ending on Sunday at 11:59 pm. The normal work schedule for all full-time employees is an eight (8) hour day, five (5) consecutive days a week beginning on Monday. All non-exempt employees can only work 40 hours per work week (including vacation, sick and annual leave time). If a non-exempt employee has more than 40 hours in a work week without prior written approval, the hours above 40 will be paid at 1½ the hourly rate; however disciplinary action may be incurred. Please reference page 27 for Partners’ disciplinary action policy. Non-Exempt Administrative Staff For any non-exempt staff charging his/her time to administrative units, no overtime is allowed. The employee and his/her supervisor will need to monitor the timesheet so the employee does not incur more than 40 hours in a work week. This means that working days may need to be adjusted per individual to accommodate late meetings, business travel, trainings, etc. to ensure they do not cross the threshold of 40 hours each work week. Non-Exempt Program Staff For any non-exempt staff charging his/her time to programs, they may be

eligible for overtime if pre-approved in writing from both his/her supervisor and the grants officer. Please note governmental grants cannot be charged any overtime per USG Regulations, however if written consent is given from the grants officer in a signed letter/memo then Partners can charge additional time over 40 hours. Any situation in which the grants officer does not approve a request for overtime and Partners believes it is critical to the success of the project; it will be reviewed on a case by case basis. If approved, the first 40 hours worked would need to be expensed to the grant and any additional hours over 40 would need to be expensed to an administrative unit. Business travel (as referenced below) cannot put an employee into overtime status, therefore working days may need to be adjusted per individual to accommodate business travel, late meetings, training, etc. to ensure they do not cross the threshold of 40 hours each work week. Non-Exempt Employees and Business Travel, Seminars and Trainings When a non-exempt employee is on travel, their travel time must be taken into consideration. Commute time to and from work (i.e. Partners’ International Office) is not considered time worked. However, when traveling during normal work hours is part of the job activity or when travel is necessary from meeting to meeting, it is time worked. When it comes to business travel this also means from the moment an employee leaves his/her house until the time they are settled into their accommodation at his/her destination, he/she must be paid for all hours traveled. Therefore Partners recommends that non-exempt staff only travel Monday-Friday and not on weekends. While on business travel, the employee should be paid for all hours that they would normally be in office. For example if an employee’s standard work week is Monday-Friday from 9AM-5PM, while on travel the employee should be paid during that time period, unless on any form of leave. While on business travel

employees will not be compensated for time sleeping or meals, unless the meal is part of a meeting, as this is not considered hours worked. However, if an employee is on business travel and has meetings/work scheduled outside of normal business hours, he/she will need to include that time as time worked on his/her timesheet. For example, an employee on travel hosts a dinner meeting from 7PM-9PM; he/she should be compensated for those two hours. They should then adjust their work schedule that week to ensure they do not exceed 40 hours. Any non-exempt employee attending lectures, meetings, training programs and similar activities are viewed as working time unless all of the following criteria are met: Attendance is outside of the employee’s regular working hours; Attendance is in fact voluntary; The course, lecture, or meeting is not directly related to the employee’s job; and The employee does not perform any productive work during such attendance. Basic rule of thumb is to consider if the company or individual is paying for the training Non-Exempt Employees and After Hours Work A non-exempt employee is never expected to work outside of their set 40 hours a week under any circumstances. This includes abstaining from working on projects at home before or after normal business hours, answering emails (via laptop, cell phone, etc.) outside of office hours while at home or late at night. If an employee continues to work after they are clocked out for the day or before they clock in, they may be subject to disciplinary action as the tasks done outside of the normal work schedule must be counted towards hours worked. Non-Exempt Employees and Work from Home & Comp Time A non-exempt employee is eligible to work from home during normal business hours during each work week. However employee must not continue to work from home after they are clocked out for the day. Non-exempt employees are not eligible for Comp Time as mentioned in the Partner’s employee handbook. Non-exempt

employees are eligible for overtime if authorization is obtained and therefore comp time is not applicable to employees in this category. Non-Exempt Employees and Timesheets A non-exempt employee must clock in and out every day at the start and end of work for all hours they are on duty. The employee must clock out if they are stopping work for at least 30 minutes, anything less is considered a break and counted as time worked. If the employee wishes to eat lunch at their desk and continues to work, these hours must be considered time worked. If an employee chooses not to take lunch breaks he/she must monitor his/her time to not cross the threshold of 40 hours. For timesheet purposes, the smallest increment in which an employee can clock in or out for is 15 minutes. Therefore all-time punches for non-exempt employee should be rounded to the nearest 15 minute marker. Supervisor’s Expectations for Non-Exempt Employees Doing Work off the Clock Basic rule: The supervisor is responsible for reviewing employee timesheets. If the supervisor knew or should have known work was being done off the clock it needs to be reflected on the timesheet. It is illegal not to pay for work performed off the clock, even if it was not previously approved and/or authorized. Employees may perform a variety of potentially compensable job-related activities during their "off-the-clock" time, such as: taking work home, making/receiving job-related telephone calls at home, working before or after regular hours, job-related "volunteer" work, however the supervisor should monitor these activities to ensure all appropriate time worked is compensated. Breaks and Lunches When it comes to non-exempt employees and lunches, you do not have to pay him/her for meal periods, nor does this time count for overtime purposes, as long as employees are completely relieved of work duties during the meal period. Short break of 5-20 minutes are offered for every 4 hours worked and therefore count as time worked.

The employee should not clock out on his/her timesheet for these breaks; however lunch breaks of 30 minutes or more will require the employee to clock in and out if they are completely relied of their duties. If the employee eats lunch at their desk and continues to work, these hours must be considered paid time worked. Effective 09/30/2016

chunks.text:
Affirmative Action: Post-Offer Invitation to Self-Identify as a Veteran (VEVRAA)
As a government contractor subject to the Vietnam Era Veterans’ Readjustment Assistance Act (VEVRAA) of 1974, [Company Name] is required to submit a report to the U.S. Department of Labor each year identifying the number of protected veterans who were newly hired, as well as the number of protected veterans who were employed. If you believe you belong to any of the categories of protected veterans listed below, please indicate so by checking the appropriate box.
Submission of this information is voluntary, and refusal to provide it will not subject you to any adverse treatment. The information provided will be used only in ways that are consistent with the VEVRAA, as amended. 
Print Name:
Print Job Title: 
I belong to the following classifications of protected veterans (choose all that apply):
Disabled Veteran
A “disabled veteran” is one of the following: 
A veteran of the U.S. military, ground, naval or air service who is entitled to compensation (or who but for the receipt of military retired pay would be entitled to compensation) under laws administered by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs; or 
A person who was discharged or released from active duty because of a service-connected disability.  
Recently Separated Veteran
A “recently separated veteran” means any veteran during the three-year period beginning on the date of such veteran’s discharge or release from active duty in the U.S. military, ground, naval or air service.  
Active Wartime or Campaign Badge Veteran
An “active duty wartime or campaign badge veteran” means a veteran who served on active duty in the U.S. military, ground, naval or air service during a war, or in a campaign or expedition for which a campaign badge has been authorized under the laws administered by the Department of Defense. 
Armed Forces Service Medal Veteran
An “Armed Forces service medal veteran” means a veteran who, while serving on active duty in the U.S. military, ground, naval or air service, participated in a U.S. military operation for which an Armed Forces service medal was awarded pursuant to Executive Order 12985.  
I am a protected veteran, but I choose not to self-identify the classifications to which I belong. 
I am NOT a protected veteran. 
If you are a disabled veteran, please indicate whether there are accommodations we could provide that would enable you to perform the essential functions of your job, including special equipment, changes in the physical layout of the job, changes in the way the job is customarily performed, provision of personal assistance services or other accommodations. This information will assist us in making reasonable accommodations for your disability. 
The information you submit will be kept confidential, except that (i) supervisors and managers may be informed about the restrictions on the work or duties of disabled veterans and about necessary accommodations; (ii) first aid and safety personnel may be informed, when and to the extent appropriate, if you have a condition that might require emergency treatment; and (iii) government officials engaged in enforcing laws administered by the Office of Federal Contract Compliance Programs, or enforcing the Americans with Disabilities Act, may be informed.

chunks.text:
EEO-1 Voluntary Self Identification Form 
The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) requires all private employers with 100 or more employees as well as federal contractors and first-tier subcontractors with 50 or more employees AND contracts of at least $50,000 complete an EEO-1 report each year. Covered employers must invite employees to self-identify gender and race for this report. 
Completion of this form is voluntary and will not affect your opportunity for employment, or the terms or conditions of your employment. This form will be used for EEO-1 reporting purposes only and will be kept separate from all other personnel records only accessed by the Human Resources department. Please return completed forms to the HR department.
If you choose not to self-identify your race/ethnicity at this time, the federal government requires [Company Name] to determine this information by visual survey and/or other available information.
NAME: __________________________________
JOB TITLE: ________________________________
DATE COMPLETED: ___________________________
GENDER:(Please check one of the options below)
_____ Male
_____ Female 
RACE/ETHNICITY:(Please check one of the descriptions below corresponding to the ethnic group with which you identify.)
___ Hispanic or Latino: A person of Cuban, Mexican, Puerto Rican, South or Central American, or other Spanish culture or origin regardless of race.
___ White (Not Hispanic or Latino): A person having origins in any of the original peoples of Europe, the Middle East or North Africa.
___ Black or African American (Not Hispanic or Latino): A person having origins in any of the black racial groups of Africa.
___ Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander (Not Hispanic or Latino): A person having origins in any of the peoples of Hawaii, Guam, Samoa or other Pacific Islands.
___ Asian (Not Hispanic or Latino): A person having origins in any of the original peoples of the Far East, Southeast Asia or the Indian Subcontinent, including, for example, Cambodia, China, India, Japan, Korea, Malaysia, Pakistan, the Philippine Islands, Thailand and Vietnam.
___ Native American or Alaska Native (Not Hispanic or Latino): A person having origins in any of the original peoples of North and South America (including Central America) and who maintains tribal affiliation or community attachment.
___ Two or more races (Not Hispanic or Latino): All persons who identify with more than one of the above five races.
___ I do not wish to disclose.

documents.folder: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Sexual Harassment/2020 Sexual Harassment - Board

chunks.text: This is to acknowledge that __________________________________________ has received the Anti-Harassment Training provided by FosterThomas on (Insert Training Date Here). The above employee will comply with all requirements set forth in the training in addition to the company’s revised policies, included below. ________________________________________________________________________________ Employee Signature Date Grievance Policy It is the policy of Partners to provide employees with the opportunity to bring work-related problems and concerns to the attention of management and to resolve such problems fairly and promptly. Any employee who feels that s/he has been treated unfairly or inequitably in regards to a condition or action affecting employment should bring the matter to the attention of his/her supervisor, or a member of Senior Management. Whistleblower Policy Employees have the duty to comply with all applicable laws and to assist Partners to ensure legal compliance. Partners encourages its employees to report improper activities in the workplace and will protect employees from retaliation for making any such report in good faith. Employees have the right to report, without suffering retaliation, any activity by the organization or its employees that the employee reasonably believes: Violates any state or federal law; Violates or amounts to noncompliance with a state or federal rule or regulation; Violates fiduciary responsibilities by a corporation to its stakeholders or employees. In addition, employees can refuse to participate in an activity that would result in a violation of state or federal statutes, or a violation or noncompliance with a state or federal rule or regulation. Employees are also protected from retaliation for having exercised any of these rights in any former employment. The whistleblower protection laws do not entitle employees to violate a confidential privilege (such as attorney-client privilege) or improperly disclose trade-secret

information. Whistleblower Complaint Procedure Any employee who suspects a violation of ethics or legal compliance is required to report the situation(s) to his/her supervisor or a member of Senior Management. Alternatively, a confidential hotline at (202) 247-9610 has been established for you to report your concern via voicemail message to an outside party. The voicemail is monitored by an HR Consultant from FosterThomas who will relay any concerns to the Ethics Committee of the Board. If the situation is life-threatening or extremely critical in nature, it needs to be reported to Senior Management as well the hotline for immediate action to be taken. Employees may also report information regarding possible unlawful activity to an appropriate government or law enforcement agency. Protection from Retaliation for Whistleblower Complaint Federal and state laws provide protection from discharge or other discriminatory actions by employers in retaliation for employees’ good faith complaints about safety and health hazards or other violations of law in the workplace and the environment. The whistleblower programs do not preempt existing state statutes and common law claims. All provisions contained in the programs are in addition to protection provided by state laws. Productive Work Environment/Sexual Harassment Partners intends to provide a work environment that is pleasant, healthful, comfortable, and free from intimidation, hostility or other offenses which might interfere with work performance. Harassment of any sort – verbal, physical, environmental – is prohibited by this policy and will not be tolerated. Moreover, Partners will not tolerate harassment by supervisors, employees, volunteers, or other non-employees who conduct business with Partners. Partners does not tolerate discrimination on the basis of sex, race, color, creed, age, religion, disability, national origin, or any other legally prohibited characteristic. Harassment based on sex or any other legally

prohibited basis is illegal and is contrary to the policy of Partners. Partners will not tolerate verbal or physical conduct which harasses, disrupts or interferes with another’s work performance or which creates an intimidating, offensive or hostile work environment. In order to avoid circumstances where harassment becomes severe or pervasive, employees should report all incidents of harassment immediately. While all forms of harassment are prohibited, Partners emphasizes that sexual harassment is specifically prohibited. Each staff member has the responsibility to ensure that the workplace is free of any form of sexual harassment. No supervisor or other employee shall threaten or insinuate, either explicitly or implicitly, that an employee’s refusal to submit to sexual advances will adversely affect the staff member’s employment, wages, advancement, assigned duties, or any other condition of employment or career development. Other sexually harassing conduct in the workplace is also prohibited. Such conduct includes, but is not limited to: Unwelcome sexual flirtations, touching, advances, or propositions; Verbal abuse of a sexual nature; Graphic or sexually suggestive comments; Sexually degrading words to describe an individual; and The display, storage or transmission of sexually suggestive objects or pictures. Harassment Complaint Procedure If you believe you have been subjected to prohibited harassment or you are aware of any such conduct, the incident must be reported immediately. We ask that any person who has witnessed or been the subject of harassment contact our independent HR Consultant, FosterThomas. A confidential hotline at (202) 247-9610 has been set up for you to report your concern via voicemail message. The voicemail is monitored by an HR Consultant from FosterThomas who will review all messages and promptly contact you to discuss your concern and to gain a full understanding of the situation. The HR Consultant will then provide an overview of the

situation to the Ethics Committee of the Board. The Ethics Committee will then review the case and make a determination of whether the case should be referred to the President for further action. If the complainant provides no identifying information in the message, FosterThomas will relay the complaint directly to the Ethics Committee for review. Alternatively, or in addition to the confidential hotline, you may report harassment to your supervisor. If your supervisor is unavailable, or if you do not feel comfortable contacting that individual, you may also contact another supervisor at Partners. Such complaints must be recorded in writing, either by the complainant or by the supervisor to whom it is reported. (An optional Harassment Complaint Form is available in the Partners HR Handbook or by request to the Finance Office.) Any supervisor who becomes aware of possible harassment or retaliation must immediately advise a member of Senior Management (President or CFO) so it can be investigated in a timely and confidential manner. All harassment complaints will be promptly and thoroughly investigated and each complaint will be handled as confidentially as possible under the circumstances. Complaints will be investigated impartially and if found to be truthful, Partners will take immediate corrective action. Anyone found to have engaged in harassment will be subject to appropriate disciplinary action up to, and including, termination of employment. Protection from Retaliation for Harassment Complaint Partners prohibits any retaliation against employees who legitimately exercise their rights under this policy or who participate in an investigation. There is to be no interference, coercion, discrimination, reprisal or retaliation against any employee for making a valid and truthful complaint or report of a violation. Employees who believe they are being discriminated against as a result of having legitimately filed a complaint or having truthfully participated in an
investigation should immediately notify a member of Senior Management or use the confidential hotline.
