No supervisor or other employee shall threaten or insinuate, either explicitly or implicitly, that an employee’s refusal to submit to sexual advances will adversely affect the staff member’s employment, wages, advancement, assigned duties, or any other condition of employment or career development. Other sexually harassing conduct in the workplace is also prohibited. Such conduct includes: a) sexual flirtations, inappropriate touching, advances, or propositions; b) verbal abuse of a sexual nature; c) graphic or sexually suggestive comments; d) sexually degrading words to describe an individual; and e) the display, storing or transmitting of sexually suggestive objects or pictures. Partners accepts no liability for harassment of one employee by another employee. The individual who makes unwelcome advances, threatens or in any way harasses another employee is personally liable for such actions and their consequences. Partners will not provide legal, financial or any other assistance to an individual accused of harassment if a legal complaint is filed. Harassment Complaint Procedure If you believe you

have been subjected to prohibited harassment or you are aware of any such conduct, the incident must immediately be reported, to your supervisor. If your supervisor is unavailable or if you do not feel comfortable contacting that individual, you should contact a senior manager of Partners. All complaints will be promptly and thoroughly investigated and each complaint will be handled as confidentially as possible. Partners ensure that employees following this complaint procedure will be protected against illegal retaliation. Complaints will be investigated impartially and if found to be truthful, Partners will take immediate corrective action. Anyone found to have engaged in harassment will be subject to disciplinary action, up to, and including termination of employment. Retaliation for Sexual Harassment Complaint If you experience or witness sexual or other unlawful harassment in the workplace, report it immediately to your supervisor. If your supervisor is unavailable or you believe it would be inappropriate to discuss it with that person, you should immediately report it to any member of management. All allegations of sexual harassment will be quickly and discreetly investigated. To the extent possible, your confidentiality and that of any witnesses and the alleged harasser will be protected against unnecessary disclosure. When the investigation is completed, you will be informed of the outcome of the investigation. Partners does not tolerate any retaliation against employees who legitimately exercise their rights under this policy or who participate in an investigation. There is to be no interference, coercion, discrimination, reprisal or retaliation against any employee for making a valid and truthful complaint or report of a violation. Employees who believe they are being discriminated against as a result of having legitimately filed a complaint or having truthfully participated in an investigation should immediately notify a member of senior management. Any
supervisor who becomes aware of possible harassment or retaliation must immediately advise a member of senior management so it can be investigated in a timely and confidential manner.

chunks.text: This is to acknowledge that __________________________________________ has received the Anti-Harassment Training provided by FosterThomas on (Insert Training Date Here). The above employee will comply with all requirements set forth in the training in addition to the company’s revised policies, included below. ________________________________________________________________________________ Employee Signature Date Grievance Policy It is the policy of Partners to provide employees with the opportunity to bring work-related problems and concerns to the attention of management and to resolve such problems fairly and promptly. Any employee who feels that s/he has been treated unfairly or inequitably in regards to a condition or action affecting employment should bring the matter to the attention of his/her supervisor, or a member of Senior Management. Whistleblower Policy Employees have the duty to comply with all applicable laws and to assist Partners to ensure legal compliance. Partners encourages its employees to report improper activities in the workplace and will protect employees from retaliation for making any such report in good faith. Employees have the right to report, without suffering retaliation, any activity by the organization or its employees that the employee reasonably believes: Violates any state or federal law; Violates or amounts to noncompliance with a state or federal rule or regulation; Violates fiduciary responsibilities by a corporation to its stakeholders or employees. In addition, employees can refuse to participate in an activity that would result in a violation of state or federal statutes, or a violation or noncompliance with a state or federal rule or regulation. Employees are also protected from retaliation for having exercised any of these rights in any former employment. The whistleblower protection laws do not entitle employees to violate a confidential privilege (such as attorney-client privilege) or improperly disclose trade-secret

information. Whistleblower Complaint Procedure Any employee who suspects a violation of ethics or legal compliance is required to report the situation(s) to his/her supervisor or a member of Senior Management. Alternatively, a confidential hotline at (202) 247-9610 has been established for you to report your concern via voicemail message to an outside party. The voicemail is monitored by an HR Consultant from FosterThomas who will relay any concerns to the Ethics Committee of the Board. If the situation is life-threatening or extremely critical in nature, it needs to be reported to Senior Management as well the hotline for immediate action to be taken. Employees may also report information regarding possible unlawful activity to an appropriate government or law enforcement agency. Protection from Retaliation for Whistleblower Complaint Federal and state laws provide protection from discharge or other discriminatory actions by employers in retaliation for employees’ good faith complaints about safety and health hazards or other violations of law in the workplace and the environment. The whistleblower programs do not preempt existing state statutes and common law claims. All provisions contained in the programs are in addition to protection provided by state laws. Productive Work Environment/Sexual Harassment Partners intends to provide a work environment that is pleasant, healthful, comfortable, and free from intimidation, hostility or other offenses which might interfere with work performance. Harassment of any sort – verbal, physical, environmental – is prohibited by this policy and will not be tolerated. Moreover, Partners will not tolerate harassment by supervisors, employees, volunteers, or other non-employees who conduct business with Partners. Partners does not tolerate discrimination on the basis of sex, race, color, creed, age, religion, disability, national origin, or any other legally prohibited characteristic. Harassment based on sex or any other legally

prohibited basis is illegal and is contrary to the policy of Partners. Partners will not tolerate verbal or physical conduct which harasses, disrupts or interferes with another’s work performance or which creates an intimidating, offensive or hostile work environment. In order to avoid circumstances where harassment becomes severe or pervasive, employees should report all incidents of harassment immediately. While all forms of harassment are prohibited, Partners emphasizes that sexual harassment is specifically prohibited. Each staff member has the responsibility to ensure that the workplace is free of any form of sexual harassment. No supervisor or other employee shall threaten or insinuate, either explicitly or implicitly, that an employee’s refusal to submit to sexual advances will adversely affect the staff member’s employment, wages, advancement, assigned duties, or any other condition of employment or career development. Other sexually harassing conduct in the workplace is also prohibited. Such conduct includes, but is not limited to: Unwelcome sexual flirtations, touching, advances, or propositions; Verbal abuse of a sexual nature; Graphic or sexually suggestive comments; Sexually degrading words to describe an individual; and The display, storage or transmission of sexually suggestive objects or pictures. Harassment Complaint Procedure If you believe you have been subjected to prohibited harassment or you are aware of any such conduct, the incident must be reported immediately. We ask that any person who has witnessed or been the subject of harassment contact our independent HR Consultant, FosterThomas. A confidential hotline at (202) 247-9610 has been set up for you to report your concern via voicemail message. The voicemail is monitored by an HR Consultant from FosterThomas who will review all messages and promptly contact you to discuss your concern and to gain a full understanding of the situation. The HR Consultant will then provide an overview of the

situation to the Ethics Committee of the Board. The Ethics Committee will then review the case and make a determination of whether the case should be referred to the President for further action. If the complainant provides no identifying information in the message, FosterThomas will relay the complaint directly to the Ethics Committee for review. Alternatively, or in addition to the confidential hotline, you may report harassment to your supervisor. If your supervisor is unavailable, or if you do not feel comfortable contacting that individual, you may also contact another supervisor at Partners. Such complaints must be recorded in writing, either by the complainant or by the supervisor to whom it is reported. (An optional Harassment Complaint Form is available in the Partners HR Handbook or by request to the Finance Office.) Any supervisor who becomes aware of possible harassment or retaliation must immediately advise a member of Senior Management (President or CFO) so it can be investigated in a timely and confidential manner. All harassment complaints will be promptly and thoroughly investigated and each complaint will be handled as confidentially as possible under the circumstances. Complaints will be investigated impartially and if found to be truthful, Partners will take immediate corrective action. Anyone found to have engaged in harassment will be subject to appropriate disciplinary action up to, and including, termination of employment. Protection from Retaliation for Harassment Complaint Partners prohibits any retaliation against employees who legitimately exercise their rights under this policy or who participate in an investigation. There is to be no interference, coercion, discrimination, reprisal or retaliation against any employee for making a valid and truthful complaint or report of a violation. Employees who believe they are being discriminated against as a result of having legitimately filed a complaint or having truthfully participated in an
investigation should immediately notify a member of Senior Management or use the confidential hotline.

chunks.text:
Productive Work Environment/Sexual Harassment 
Partners intends to provide for its employees, and non-employees (such as interns, volunteers, consultants and independent contractors), a work environment that is pleasant, healthful, comfortable, and free from intimidation, hostility or other offenses which might interfere with their performance. Harassment of any sort – verbal, physical, environmental – is prohibited by this policy and will not be tolerated. Moreover, Partners will not tolerate harassment by any employee, non-employee or member of the Partners network. 
Partners does not tolerate discrimination on the basis of sex, race, color, creed, age, religion, disability, national origin, or any other legally prohibited characteristic. Harassment based on sex or any other legally prohibited basis is illegal and is contrary to the policy of Partners. 
Partners will not tolerate verbal or physical conduct which harasses, disrupts or interferes with work performance or which creates an intimidating, offensive or hostile work environment. In order to avoid circumstances where harassment becomes severe or pervasive, employees and non-employees should report all incidents of harassment immediately. While all forms of harassment are prohibited, Partners emphasizes that sexual harassment is specifically prohibited. Each employee and non-employee has the responsibility to ensure that the workplace is free of any form of sexual harassment. No Partners employees, non-employees or members of the Partners network shall threaten or insinuate, either explicitly or implicitly, that any person’s refusal to submit to sexual advances will adversely affect the work.
Other sexually harassing conduct in the workplace is also prohibited. Such conduct includes, but is not limited to: a) unwelcome sexual flirtations, touching, advances, or propositions; b) verbal abuse of a sexual nature; c) graphic or sexually suggestive comments; d) sexually degrading words to describe an individual; and e) the display, storage or transmission of sexually suggestive objects or pictures. 
Harassment Complaint Procedure 
If you believe you have been subjected to prohibited harassment or you are aware of any such conduct, the incident must be reported immediately, preferably in writing, to any supervisor or Senior Manager of Partners of the Americas. All complaints will be promptly and thoroughly investigated and each complaint will be handled as confidentially as possible under the circumstances. Partners prohibits retaliation against any person who files a harassment complaint or cooperates with an investigation. Complaints will be investigated impartially and if found to be truthful, Partners will take immediate corrective action. Anyone found to have engaged in harassment will be subject to appropriate disciplinary action up to, and including termination. 
Retaliation for Harassment Complaint 
Partners prohibits any retaliation against anyone who legitimately exercise their rights under this policy or who participate in an investigation. There is to be no interference, coercion, discrimination, reprisal or retaliation against any person for making a valid and truthful complaint or report of a violation. Any employees or non-employees who believe they are being discriminated against as a result of having legitimately filed a complaint or having truthfully participated in an investigation should immediately notify a member of management. Anyone who becomes aware of possible harassment or retaliation must immediately advise Partners of the Americas so it can be investigated in a timely and confidential manner.
This policy applies to all individuals when interacting with members of the Partners staff or network.
I have read and agree to this policy.

chunks.text: Statement of Confidentiality of Corporate Affairs The nature of Partners’ work makes it essential that employees or representatives do not divulge proprietary company information unless expressly authorized by an officer of the company. All inquiries about Partners’ corporate policies or details of our business or work products are to be referred to or reviewed with an officer of the company. Unless authorized by a company officer, employees or representatives may not reveal details of Partners’ clientele and contracts. As required by FAR 52.203-18, FAR 52.203-19, and FAR 3.909, Partners does not require employees, representatives, and contractors to sign internal confidentiality agreements that could prevent or impede the reporting of fraud, waste, or abuse to appropriate authorities. Employees, representatives, and contractors are encouraged to report concerns, suspicions, or allegations of fraud, waste, abuse, or other misconduct to their supervisor, any member of management, HR, or to Foster Thomas at bbulger@fosterthomas.com. Anonymous reporting options are available and will be treated confidentially to the extent permitted by law. Partners prohibits retaliation against any employee or representative who reports or participates in the investigation of suspected fraud, waste, abuse, or any other misconduct in violation of laws, regulations, or company policies. Use of Proprietary and Confidential Information Partners’ employees and its representatives may have access to and become acquainted with proprietary and confidential information such as client lists, rates, financial records, internal structure, internal policies, publications, inventories, computer software and related documentation and similar information, which is owned by Partners and which is regularly used in the operation of the business of the company. I agree not to use such proprietary or confidential information obtained during employment or contractual relationship with Partners as a means

of making private profit. Nondisclosure of Proprietary or Confidential Information I recognize and acknowledge that certain information concerning Partners’ business is a valuable, special and unique asset of the company. I agree not to disclose Partners’ proprietary or confidential information to any person or entity for any reason, except as required in the course of employment or contractual relationship, or required by law or court order; and further agree not to make use of such information for my own benefit or the private benefit of others. Ownership of Property All files, records, documents, drawings, specifications, equipment and similar items relating to the business of Partners, including computer software or software documentation, whether prepared by me or otherwise coming into my access or possession, shall remain the exclusive property of Partners and shall not be reproduced, removed or transmitted from the premises of the company under any circumstances without the prior written consent of the company. Upon termination of employment with Partners, I will return all company property to my supervisor or a company officer. I agree that if any trade secret, formula, device, technology, technical information, process, etc. (“invention”) is made by me during the course of my employment or contractual relationship, alone or jointly with others which is made with Partners’ equipment, supplies, facilities, trade secrets or time, or which relates to the business of Partners, or which results from any work performed by me for Partners, such invention will belong to Partners and Partners will have full authority to use, transfer or license it. I agree that Partners will have the right to keep such invention as a trade secret, if it so chooses. I further agree to assign to Partners my rights in any invention where the company is required to grant those rights to the United States Government. It is further agreed that Partners is not required to designate me as

the inventor or author of any such invention if it is distributed publicly or otherwise. I understand that violation of this policy is cause for immediate dismissal or termination of contractual relationship and/or demand for financial restitution to Partners for damages to the limit allowed by law. I have read and agree to this policy. Print Name Employee SignatureDate

chunks.text:
Personnel Change Process
The employee submits changes for name, address and/or benefits to F&A Office by utilizing the Status Change Form.
The supervisor submits changes in the employee’s job title, department, rate of pay and job status to F&A Office by utilizing the Status Change Form.
F&A Office records the change in the Paychex HR/Payroll system(s) and places a copy of the Status Change Form in the employee’s personnel file.  
F&A Office updates changes with the benefits carriers, if applicable.

chunks.text: Full Telecommuting Arrangement Request Part 1- Employee Request for Full Telecommuting Arrangement Please complete this application form to request a full telecommuting arrangement. This completed form must be signed by your direct supervisor, VP of HR, and President & CEO to be in effect. Personal Details Employee Name: Ana Aslan Unit: Labor Job Title: Director Direct Supervisor: Macarena Jimenez Granda Direct Reports (if any): Summary of Job Responsibilities: Oversees the implementation of labor rights projects in the region. Provides technical guidance for addressing international labor issues to field projects and the Labor Team. Start Date: February 2024 End Date: City, State, and Country where telework will be performed: Silver Spring, MD Request Type: New Continuation FLSA Status: Non-exempt Exempt Provide reason(s) for the proposed full telecommuting arrangement: I am requesting this arrangement based the following reasons: I have been working very effectively from my home office since 2017. It is a fully equipped office, with high-speed internet, photocopy machine, scanner, and all the tools needed to conduct my job effectively. My team is fully teleworking from different parts of the area and the world (Paraguay, Philadelphia, Baltimore), and the people that I interact daily are in Mexico and Colombia. I do not work closely with anybody based in the Home Office. Therefore, I spend my workdays there by myself, barely interacting with others. Given my introspective nature, just physically being in the office does not stimulate my integration or coordination with other teams, propels the flow of new ideas, or forges new connections. I see the value of participating in person during monthly meetings and office-wide activities. I would certainly go into the office during those dates. Provide details of how this arrangement would benefit Partners of the Americas: I would keep performing at a high level as I have been doing since entering the organization. Not

having the extra stress from going to the office will be beneficial for my wellbeing, which will be reflected in the commitment to my work. Provide details of any job responsibilities (including staff management, if applicable) that could/would be affected by a full telecommuting arrangement: None Provide any additional information, if any, that should be taken into consideration when evaluating this request: Flex Work & Telecommuting Expectations: I understand: A telecommuting arrangement may be discontinued at will and at any time at the request of either the employee or Partners. Telecommuting employees are expected to comply with all Partners policies and procedures and maintain standards for performance, work hours, workload, meeting attendance, and required deliverables. Telecommuting employees may still be expected to return to the Home Office and/or other specified location(s) for certain meetings and organization-wide events, among other reasons, as determined by the direct supervisor or President & CEO. Every effort will be made to provide ample notice. Telecommuting employees must be available with high-speed internet, sufficient for high-quality video conferencing, when working. Telecommuting employees may not accept any other employment or non-Partners work assignments during work hours. Telecommuting arrangements are not a substitute for childcare or other dependent care. Employee Signature: __________________________________ Date: ______________ Direct Supervisor Decision: Approve ☐ Deny ☐ Direct Supervisor Signature: _____________________________ Date: ______________ ELT Decision: Approve ☐ Deny ☐ ​President/CEO Signature: _______________________________ Date: ______________ Part 2 – Supervisor, HR, ELT Evaluation Notes Please describe the employee’s performance, attitude, knowledge and skills, and length of employment at Partners Please describe any of the employee’s job duties that may be affected by a full telecommuting arrangement. How would

Partners mitigate the potential adverse effects? chunks.text: Partners of the Americas Total Compensation Package Survey - Comments Perception of POA’s Pay Scale Comments: Program office pay could be higher I know others with a similar role as mine who get paid more than the range POA has set for a person in my ranking. That is to say that the range for my ranking (position) is slightly higher than other places. However, the roles in those places require much more responsibility. I’m not familiar with the pay scale Process for promotions could be improved to make up for less competitive salary scale I was hired at $10k under the rate for my position at similar orgs with my experience level and at over $20k below the going rate for my position in Washington DC I believe that employees should be able to be part of their salary negotiations (sit at the negotiation table) so that they can feel represented and heard. I think that promotions and salary adjustments cannot just be based on the timeframe a person has been in the organization, but rather a combination of many factors such as performance, otherwise it takes away incentive of doing a good job The pay scale is not competitive enough for the DC cost of living. The pay is not good and I am looking for a new job – POA is great, but the pay, lack of trust by my unit director, and continued lies about international field opportunities has worn me down and I’m very unhappy. I don’t feel this person can change and I no longer enjoy my job. I don’t wish to speak to HR, no one else in my unit has these issues. I just want to go to work somewhere else. The lack of value placed on my skillset by my supervisor is apparent to others on my team and I don’t enjoy coming to work anymore. Everyone is so nice to work with, but I don’t need the unnecessary stress. While I believe that POA’s pay scale is good, if we want to grow and attract the talent we need to grow, we need to be more competitive in pay scale. Also, while we want to compare POA with other similar orgs, we need to think that

we not only compete with the same to attract talent, but with others bigger and more competitive than us including but not limited to Winrock, FHI 360, and even for-profit companies, Chemonics, etc. I am mostly happy with my salary, but it would be great to have more communication on the opportunities for promotions/bonuses I haven’t worked at any other NGOs in the DC area, so it’s hard to know how our pay scale compares to others, but my general impression is that Partners is middle of the stream for NGOs and low when compared to the private sector Perception of POA’s Health Insurance Comments: Didn't respond to #1 b/c I already stated salary was most important, however, health insurance is still important to me Insurance is okay I would like there to be more options, especially other ones from Kaiser for people who can’t use Kaiser for whatever reasons (ex: specialist or doctor out of network). The other options are much more expensive/increase a lot annually. I do not use the POA health insurance because U am fully covered under my spouse’s plan at zero cost. I would like to suggest though, that POA consider offering a cash stipend in place of the health insurance benefit for people in my position. The cash stipend could be fixed amount per month or a percentage of what coverage would have cost. I have not heard of a number of other companies and organizations offering this type of compensation in place of the health insurance benefit. It is frustrating not to be able to take advantage of what is perhaps the greatest dollar value benefit POA offers when I know that my abstaining from enrolling in the insurance plan saves the organization a considerable amount of money and the insurance still appears in my annual compensation breakdown as if I were getting it. Perception of POA’s Vision Insurance Comments: Vision benefit is okay, but really hard to find providers who accept it 4: I haven’t used it yet, so not sure if I’m satisfied yet Vision insurance is okay I

have not used this yet, but probably will in the future I also do not use this benefit because I am covered by my spouse’s plan Perception of POA’s Dental Insurance Comments: This is something I use once a year and while it’s a good plan (I pay nothing, practically), the nearest dentist is quite far from where I live. That is to say not many options to choose from within the city. 4: I haven’t used it yet, so unsure Would like to be back on the previous plan so that my current dentist can accept it. I’m using a secondary insurance to stay with my current dentist I am happy with the dental insurance, but it could include orthodontics. Maybe in the future! The rate of the co=pay is awesome for the yearly cleaning. I also do not use this benefit because I am covered by my spouse’s plan. Perception of POA’s Optional Short-Term Disability/Life Insurance Comments: I have a disability so this is very important to me and, unfortunately, we are often underprotected in the workplace 4: I don’t have it, but I still value that I have the option to select it should I want it in the future I do not have this insurance. I don’t believe I have one of these policies. If 100% of the cost is covered by the employee, I don’t really see how this is a benefit at all. Perception of POA’s Life Insurance Comments: I don’t think I have this insurance. Perception of POA’s Long Term Disability Insurance Comments: While I appreciate this, I would prefer a benefit more conducive to my age bracket, perhaps better benefits for fitness and wellness centers. I’d prefer is the health benefits were more preventative at my age. Perception of POA’s Retirement Plan Comments: Should begin as soon as employed, not after 1 year The “IDK” answers would depend on salary range. Meaning, if I feel comfortable with my salary, then the percentage for retirement won’t be impacted so much POA match could increase after each additional year employed The plan and match should commence when employee commences work,

without the one-year waiting period Perception of POA’s Annual Leave (AL) Policy Comments: 15 days of AL is not competitive. In addition, POA staff work so hard until they get overwhelmed. If POA offers 3-5 days upfront of AL, without the need of accruals, then POA staff may be less dissatisfied with that. Would be nice to have more AL days within a year and have a higher number of days roll into the new year. There should be more AL days provided to employees with longer tenure at Partners. It should not stop at 3 years. AL is a critical benefit and among the most important for maintaining a good work/life balance. In addition, I greatly appreciated the new policy allowing staff to have more flexibility in small fluctuations in their hours (I.e. not having to use AL for blocks of time less than 3 hours). This helps preserve AL for real vacations, while still having the day-to-day flexibility to deal with issues that require small amounts of time off from work. Perception of POA’s Sick Leave (SL) Policy Comments: I suggest looking into a policy that offers no SL and higher days of AL, that way employees can use AL if they are sick, but if not, they can use it for vacation If possible, it would be great to have an opportunity to carry more days into a new year to accumulate time. Also, after 5 years, can we gain more annual leave? Perception of POA’s Sick Leave Bank Policy Comments: Should be able to use as leave SL bank is not clear in the EM. Employees want to use SL they receive from others without a reasonable leave, at this moment we don’t know if we can use what is donated or not. Very fuzzy policy that could help many of us without hurting the organization. I rarely use SL so it’s not something I value too much. If I have a doctor’s appointment, I just make up the hours within the week by working extra. I would much rather trade in SL for more AL. I’m unsure of exactly how the sick bank works, but should there be a cut-off? I don’t think it makes sense for

employees to rack up SL that they would never use. I think this is a great benefit The sick leave bank compensates for tangible benefits. This is very important because if something happens and you need to be out of the office for medical reasons, you are provided with stability I hope I don’t ever have to use it, but it’s a considerable comfort to know that the many hours I’ve accrued in my sick bank are there in case I end up with some long-term debilitation or illness. Perception of POA’s Sick Leave Donation Policy Comments: I would suggest to change this to a SL that could be used for someone in need of SL. Ex: new staff who does not have SL accrued This is something that I don’t tap into. Couldn’t find this under employee manual for 2018 I think this policy should be reexamined...seems like a big loophole... I like the policy, but perhaps it should be more limited The sick leave donation is especially important to cover for a short maternity leave. POA’s maternity/paternity is okay, but to be honest, quite short for women giving birth, especially considering complications, c-section, among others, and this donation permits one to have the flexibility to extend much needed time up to 2-3 months total. It is a very attractive approach to cover for a need that is trending and receiving more importance worldwide. I really like this policy and wish more people knew about it. I did not know about this until a few months ago. The sick leave donation policy is wonderful, however it shouldn’t be considered a substitute for other types of leave policies (I.e. employees shouldn’t have to depend on the goodwill of their colleagues in order to take a reasonable period of paid parental leave) I think this is a very human policy that is good for individuals who benefit and for the group culture. It encourages, or at least gives people the option, to help out their colleagues in a time of need. I think this is a very important benefit, even if it is not used very often.

Perception of POA’s Parental Leave Policy Comments: 4 weeks seems really short Yes for paternity leave, not enough for maternity leave Although this does not apply to me, I would like to see more days/weeks for maternity/paternity leave This should be longer if possible All research shows that 4 weeks is not nearly long enough for parental leave, and leaves employees with limited options: hope colleagues will donate sick leave (never guaranteed, and you don’t know how much you may receive), leave a child that is far too young in daycare when the mother also may not be fully recovered, or make difficult financial decisions to take unpaid leave under FMLA. While POA is based in the US, that doesn’t mean it needs to reflect the outdated parental leave policies of this country, instead it should look to the countries where we work throughout Latin America for inspiration for a more human policy that truly reflects the values of the organization. On question 4 regarding the number of days of parental leave available, I think it’s important to note that POA is indeed exceptional when compared with other US employers in that it provides any parental leave at all, but I believe strongly that employers in general should be providing more parental leave, including POA. So I rank POA highly on this score when compared with their peers, but I also don’t think it would be inappropriate to offer 2-4 months of parental leave Perception of POA’s Compassionate Leave Policy Comments: Thankfully, I haven’t had to use these days. 3 days is sufficient Would be great to provide more time for this leave considering travel days involved in attending a funeral, and coming back to the office. 5 days could be more sufficient. Perhaps there should be tiered system. Three days off for the death of a spouse or child seems too little. I fortunately have not had need of this benefit, but I think it would be great relief after losing a family member to not have to worry about work for a few days. I

believe this is also a good manifestation of POA’s intention of cultivating a family-friendly work culture. Perception of POA’s Paid Holidays Comments: Thank you for all the paid holidays! Should be able to have the option of floating holiday to respect all cultures. You work during a holiday you don’t celebrate and can take another day off as a floating holiday. I appreciate this benefit. However, it would be more effective if time off including holidays and weekends were respected by some supervisors. Sometimes the pressure is too much that we feel obligated to work over the clock until it surpasses our limits, causing so much mental stress. Making note that this year Father’s Day had early dismissal while Mother’s Day didn’t. Would like to encourage equal early dismissal for both holidays, if that generosity were to happen again. Whenever a paid holiday is approaching and/or when we return from a holiday, would be great to consider how these holidays impact workload and deadlines. For example, holding meetings the day after returning from a holiday isn’t ideal. Also, setting deadlines and program activities in the days before or after are also not ideal. Often times, I find myself working on holidays to account for these demands. It would be good to get voting day off so everyone has a chance to vote. In addition to the planned paid holidays, POA often gives a few additional days each year, such as the day after Christmas or Thanksgiving, especially when it helps the holiday bridge to a weekend. This is really wonderful. I would suggest, however, that such closures be announced with a bit more notice than is currently typical. Several times it has happened that I have scheduled meeting on days that are then announced as holidays. I usually end up taking the meetings anyway. If the closure were announced a little more in advance (say one week at least), then that would help me avoid that situation or at least have more time to reschedule meetings. Perception of

POA’s Flex Spending Account Comments: I wish the FSA were more open to covering costs such as emergency birth control and allergy medication. This may be outside the scope of the FSA benefit and more of an issue related to FSA in general. Is it really 40%? That seems high to me. It really saves you the amount of your effective tax rate, I believe, because you are really just getting the money pre-tax. I doubt anyone in the office pays a 40% effective tax rate. It's not really important for the purposes of this survey, but I thought I’d mention it in case that figure is somehow used in our financial statements or something. Perception of POA’s Smart Benefits Comments: I believe POA could pay a % of transportation to staff (eg. 30% or 20%) Some employers provide their employees $x amount on a monthly basis. That’s cool but if this costs more than allotting an extra AL day, then the AL day would be better. This is a benefit that could easily be improved and help people commuting from long distances Wish smart cards were connected to Uber. Sometimes Uber is a commute arrangement that works better when buses and train schedules are undergoing adjustments. Again, this suggestion is probably outside the scope of POA to address. Can this extend to bicycle repair costs? Transportation subsidy would be helpful It would be great for Partners to cover more of the SmartTrip benefits especially for those who have longer commutes – it can get expensive Other NGOs I’ve worked at provided a reimbursement for approved travel expenses. The PAS policy is less generous and far more complex. Again, it’s probably not really 40%. Also, I have heard of other DC area employers offering cash benefits for commuting or for parking, in addition to the Smart Benefits program. Perception of POA’s Flex Work Arrangement Policy Comments: Awesome flex work arrangement policy Wish turnaround time to process requests were faster and that the FWA arrangement went beyond a couple of months before

revisiting for adjustments I would like to see how flexible of a policy we are willing to make this as someone interested in working remotely. I’m glad that we have this option! The whole process in my unit is stressful so I don’t participate I am very happy this was added and it would benefit everyone if it stays I think the policy likely needs a bit of time to see how it plays out and for management to adapt to more modern ways of working Perception of POA’s Compensatory Time (Comp Time) Policy Comments: Still seems like employees are overworked in these cases. One day comp doesn’t seem sufficient They should accumulate/accrue as comp day used whenever Comp time at Partners is unclear/unfair. Many staff work during the weekend on events and in exchange, we receive only 1 day of comp day. It’s like taking 2 days of our rest in exchange of 1 day of compensatory time. My suggestion is that working on weekend in a conference should count 2 comp days or for each 6 day of “special event” = 1 comp day. Today, staff work for 2 weeks and receive 1 comp (?) Sometimes staff travel for 3 weeks straight and to only receive 1 day of CT? It should be 1 day of CT for every 6 days worked...or when working on a holiday I think you should receive more than 1 comp day if you travel for 12 or more days, should depend on duration of extra time worked Current policy doesn’t encourage working on a proposal that requires continuous late nights or work on weekends. Also, doesn’t take into account extra hours of work needed to catch up on regular work once proposal is submitted. Comp time is great! Wish we could have more comp days for time taken to work on things like the week before 2+ day work event because of all the final logistics leading up to the event. Wish we could have within a month rather than 2 weeks' time to take comp or simply accumulate comp days for later use when there is a slowdown in workload. Sometimes comp days are hard to take because there is no slowdown. I really

appreciate this policy I think this policy has to be reviewed and elaborated/clarified further. For example, what if staff have to work over a holiday? Or travel is extensive (I.e. 2 weeks or more)? Do they still receive 1 comp day? In these cases, they should receive more. I think this policy has to be updated and fleshed out. There are periods of work where 8 hours is not a realistic compensation (long trips, exceptionally strenuous proposal development). Additionally, I think there are some inconsistencies in its practice where some employees get comp days for working holidays when others don’t and work almost every holiday. Additionally, there are more instance where I think we should get comp days (working holidays, report development) The part about “1) staff who attend the convention” above needs to be updated It needs to be better evaluated on a work hours schedule, x amount of outside worked hours = x amount of comp time or overtime pay The policy could be a bit more flexible with comp time related to travel, especially when travel requires extensive preparation, such as giving presentations, hosting meetings, etc. Very important, especially after long trips. I would perhaps suggest that comp days be offered when a trip includes part or all of a weekend, in addition to the day offered due to the length of a trip. Perception of POA’s Professional Development Opportunities Comments: I would say better training within someone’s role would be key...then PD. If someone isn’t properly trained in their role, then THAT should be professional development. Did not know this was included in package I haven’t seen professional development opportunities come to fruition...they are discussed, but I’d like to see more follow-through I don’t think this benefit or process to request it is clearly articulated at an office-wide level Wish we could take classes and have them paid for. Wish promised trainings actually occurred more regularly I have not received much

professional development since working at Partners nor have I been encouraged by leadership to pursue them, much of it because of lack of funding. I would like it if Partners can look into this further. Professional development is stressful in my unit and does not come to fruition specifically for me, this is why I’m looking for a new job. I’ve been continually told and “lead” on and I’m over it. I’m actively applying to go work somewhere else. In my experience, POA has not made it very clear to staff what funding or other resources are available for professional development each year. In some limited circumstances, I have requested certain professional development support, which was granted, but I don’t feel that the organization is very proactive in making resources available to staff. I would suggest that a clear dollar amount be set aside annually for each employee to use of professional development, with the approval of their supervisor, of course. I believe that if staff knew there was a set amount of money available to them, they would be more proactive in seeking opportunities for their own development, which would benefit POA through their expanded skills and knowledge. Perception of POA’s 30-Minute Paid Lunch Policy Comments: Should be 1 hr/45 min Felt like this was not clarified enough in manual! Could be an hour This is interesting because my previous job had a 1-hour paid lunch and the office hours were from 9am-5pm. This ended in working 7 hrs/day for 35 hrs/week Again this is another stressful point within my unit director and I’m looking for a new job. I wish that if we come in at 9am and only take 30 min for lunch, we can leave at 5pm instead of still staying until 5:30pm Perception of POA’s Total Compensation Package Comments: Some benefit could definitely benefit more staff without hurting the org. AL for example, and also the use of SL bank donation right away. 15 days of SL is not competitive at all and even when staff is on SL, our time is not

respected. Parental leave, flex work, and professional development don’t factor in to my job. Partner’s package is fine, but during my most recent stint job-hunting, it was the least competitive package offered to me, by quite a wide margin. I accepted the position at Partners for other (personal and personal interest in particular projects) reasons, but I think it’s important to know that other NGOs do offer more competitive packages. While Partners has many fantastic qualities, is they can’t, in time, offer more competitive packages, they will eventually lose their most important asset, which is the staff. DC is an expensive area and while we would all love to work purely for the satisfaction of helping others and doing good, it isn’t realistic to think that is enough to keep people from moving on.

chunks.text:
ASM Notes for Total Compensation Package Survey
Primary Goal: To understand employee perception of POA’s current total compensation package and which benefits employees value the most. This will help Senior Leadership and HR design a compensation package and retention plan that is responsive to employee needs and desires.
Only full-time staff should take the survey – consultants, interns, and others not receiving the current total compensation package should not fill out a survey.
The survey will take approximately 25-30 minutes to complete. When you are done, place the survey in the box located in Carmen Sepassi’s office.
This survey is completely anonymous. Nobody will know who said what and nobody will try to find out who said what. If you do not want to handwrite comments in the comment boxes, feel free to type your comments, print them, and staple them to the survey.
Please be honest. Give your real opinion. The data we get from the survey is useless if everyone does not answer honestly.
Please answer every single prompt.
For each prompt you will circle only one option (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, N/A, IDK).
If you don’t know, circle IDK; if it does not apply to you, circle N/A.
We have defined some important terms, but if you do not understand something, please come ask me.
Similar NGOs – Many prompts ask that you compare POA to similar NGOs in Washington, D.C. Please note that for the purpose of this survey, consider the term “similar NGO” means international development nonprofits in the Washington, D.C. area with 20-50 HQ employees and a fringe benefits rate of approximately 27%. In other words, for the purpose of this survey, “similar NGO” does not necessarily mean one of POA’s “direct competitors.” 
Total Compensation Package – This includes all of the benefits that full-time POA employees receive: Salary, Healthcare, Vision, Dental, Short-Term Disability, Life Insurance, Retirement Plan, Annual Leave, Sick Leave, Sick Bank, Sick Leave Donation, Parental Leave, Compassionate Leave, Professional Development, 10 Paid Holidays, Flex Work Arrangement, Flex Spending Account, Smart Benefits, 30-Minute Paid Lunch. 
Competitive – Many prompts ask if a given benefit is competitive when compared to similar NGOs. For the purpose of this survey, the term “competitive” means that the benefit is roughly the same (perhaps a little higher/better or a little lower/worse) to what similar NGOs offer.

chunks.heading: POA currently pays 80% of the premium for employee and dependent dental insurance. This means, on average, POA pays between $31 and $111 per employee per month and employees pay  between $7 and $22 per month toward the insurance premium, depending on a given employee’s selected plan.

documents.path: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Trainings/Discipline/Partners Disciplinary Policy.docx
documents.folder: HR/HR Policies & Employee Manual/Trainings/Discipline

chunks.text:
Disciplinary Actions
Partners  may decide in its sole and complete discretion to warn, discipline, suspend or discharge employees with or without progressive disciplinary actions.  The appropriate action will depend on the severity of the problem, the employee’s past performance or the needs of the business.  
Unacceptable behavior, which does not lead to immediate dismissal, may be addressed with a verbal or written warning. Written warnings will include the reasons for the manager’s dissatisfaction and any supporting evidence. You will have an opportunity to defend your actions and rebut the opinion of your manager at the time the warning is issued. Disciplinary actions may also include suspensions or other measures deemed appropriate to the circumstances.
All pertinent facts will be carefully reviewed, and the employee will be given a full opportunity to explain his or her conduct before any decision is reached. The President or another member of senior management will give a second opinion concerning the unacceptable behavior before dismissal occurs.
Reasons for Disciplinary Action
In establishing rules of conduct, the company has no intention of restricting the personal rights of any individual.  Rather, we wish to define the guidelines that protect the rights of all employees and to ensure maximum understanding and cooperation. The following list of work rules is an illustration of the type of behavior that will not be tolerated by Partners. This list is not all-inclusive. Any employee who violates any of the work rules listed below may be subject to disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment.  The level of disciplinary action taken will be determined at management’s discretion and judged by the severity of the violation:
Failure to comply with Partners’ procedures, policies or practices.
Submission of false, misleading or incomplete information to obtain employment.
Failure to comply with Partners’ Confidentiality Policy.
Theft or inappropriate removal or possession of Partners’ property.
Falsification of timekeeping records.
Negligence or improper conduct leading to damage of employer-owned or customer-owned property.
The use, influence, purchase or sale of illicit drugs, controlled dangerous substances or alcohol on Partners property or a client’s site during working hours.  
Possession of weapons, explosives or firearms on company property.
Committing violent acts or threats of violence.
Committing acts of illegal discrimination or harassment. 
Insubordination, including refusal to perform assigned tasks or the performance of assigned tasks in an inappropriate manner.
Violation of safety or health rules.
Excessive absenteeism and/or tardiness without a proper excuse.
Failure to meet performance standards.
Failure to communicate and work well with others.

chunks.text:
Time Management for Partners of the Americas – Story Line
12:00pm – Opening slide 
“The biggest shortage among employed people today is time” – Brian Tracy
Introduction – quote – In a survey of 104 CEO’s were asked what specific qualities would most mark a young person in the companies for rapid promotion.  They were given a list of 50 qualities and behaviors to choose from.  84% of them agreed that 2 of the 50 qualities’ were more important than any of the others. 1) The ability to separate the relevant from the irrelevant.  The ability to set priorities. 2) The ability to get the job done fast.  It was the ability to take the ball and run with it without hesitation or delay.  The actions aimed at accomplishing the most important tasks. 
Flip chart the items/topics the group would like to cover by the end of the meeting
12:10 – flip chart the reasons for not getting things done
12:15 – 12:45  (estimate) video clip on time management in the office, recap of the video
May be removed/shortened if need more time on other sections
12:45 – 1:00 – Break
1:00 – Bio of Brian Tracy –xi Eat that Frog!
1:05 – Lists – Why we need them – Daily, weekly and monthly
Power of Lists –  25% increase efficiently - iDoneThis.com 
Plan every day the night before
Know your prime time
Eat the ugly frogs first – 80/20 rule – Pareto Principle – 20% of what you do will account for 80% of the value of what you do., 80% of the reasons that you are not achieving is contained within yourself or your company.  Only 20% of the constraints on your ability to accomplish what you want are contained in external circumstances or the environment around you. 
Do it Now!  W. Clement Stone Story  - pg 163 Time Power
W Clement Stone built a insurance fortune with more than $800 million.  In his book Success through a Positive Mental Attitude, he wrote that the repetition of the affirmation “Do it now!” was a key factor in hi rise from poverty to great wealth.  
Conscious affirmations and mental pictures become new commands for your subconscious and become your new operating principles.
A, B, C, D, E
Handout – Sample list formats
Five Questions for Setting Priorities
Definitions of A, B, C, D,E
1:20 – Goals
Short Term, Long Term
How to write them
Do something Daily towards your goals
Exercise – write top three goals
1:30 – Staying Motivated
What does your desk look like? – Direct Mail Entrepreneur Joe Sugarman –“ End every day with a clean desk”       -Filing systems
       -Sample flow chart for processing paper
Interruptions
       -Samples of how to handle interruptions
Procrastination
       -16 areas to overcome procrastination
- Positive attitudes - Rewards
Handouts – Alternative workspaces
16 areas to overcome procrastination 
Flowchart for processing paperwork
Samples of how to handle interruptions
1:50 – Revisit flip charts and confirm issues/topics were covered. 
2:00 – Seminar complete
Parting motivational quote given to each participant
Reference Books:
Eat that Frog!  21 Great Ways to Stop Procrastinating and Get More Done in Less Time – Brian Tracy
Time Power,  A Proven System for Getting More Done in Less Time Than You Ever Thought Possible – Brian Tracey
Getting Things Done, The  Art of Stress Free Productivity - David Allen

documents.path: HR/Insurance & Benefits Information/Flex-Spending and Dependent Care - TASC/FAQ for FSA-DCA Benefits.docx
documents.folder: HR/Insurance & Benefits Information/Flex-Spending and Dependent Care - TASC

chunks.text:
Flex-Spending & Dependent Care (FSA)
Using a flexible spending account (FSA), you can pay for your health and day care expenses and save money at the same time! 
Do I have to participate in my company’s FSA?
No. You are under no obligation to participate in an FSA. You are only required to sign an election form and indicate your choice not to participate.
What advantages does a flexible spending account (FSA) offer me?
With an FSA, you save money by paying for qualified benefits using pre-tax dollars. FSA funds are deducted from your paycheck (gross earnings) before taxes are taken out. This means you are taxed on a lower gross salary and can end up with more take-home pay.
Overall, this can add up to a savings of up to 30%-40% on your out-of-pocket expenses! Calculate your own potential savings here: www.fsafeds.com/fsafeds/fsa_calculator.asp 
What happens to the money that I put into the FSA?
You will be issued a FSA debit card that can be used to pay for qualifying expenses at participating retailers and service providers.  Any expenses not paid on your debit card can be submitted for reimbursement by check.  FSA funds can be spent at any point during the year, up to the maximum you specified; you do not need to wait until the funds “accrue.”
What benefits can I pay for with FSA pre-tax dollars?
Two categories of expenses may be reimbursed by FSAs: 
Qualified medical expenses not covered by insurances or other FSAs
Eligible dependent care expenses that allow the employee and spouse to work (spouse may be a full-time student). 
For a more detailed list, visit: www.fsafeds.com/fsafeds/eligibleexpenses.asp 
May I enroll in the FSA whenever I want?
No. You can sign up either upon employment or at the beginning of each calendar year. You may be able to enroll at other times during the plan year if you have experienced a specific change in family status (qualifying event). The minimum is $100/year to participate.
May I change or terminate my FSA at any time during the plan year?
You cannot change your FSA plan during the plan year unless you experience a qualifying event. This could be in the form of legal marital status, number of dependents, beginning or termination of employment by you, your spouse or dependent, change in work schedule, or your dependent satisfying or ceasing to satisfy eligibility requirements.
What happens to my FSA funds at the end of the year?
If you choose to enroll, you will be allowed a $500.00 carry over into the next year. By law, we can’t refund you for unused benefits. You can avoid this by using the worksheet on the back of the enrollment form to estimate your total qualifying expenses. Or try this online version: www.kiplinger.com/tools/flex/

Only insurance provisions that the individual must be aware of should be provided – allowing RSM to pass these details directly to the staff member. Where situations are serious, then RSM will activate the organization to alert them to the situation. Medical Insurance Details Insurance Carrier Name AIG Travel Guard GTP 0009158541-A Coverages Business Travel Accident Insurance Policy Number 9158541 Website aig.com/us/travelguardassistance Phone Number +1 240-864-9152 Email ShaerJN@ecsinsure.com Client Manager John Shaer Email assistance@aig.com Please provide other time-sensitive insurance provider details [if applicable]: Insurance Carrier Name Coverages General Policy Number Website Phone Number Email Client Manager Email Insurance Carrier Name Coverages Dental Policy Number Website Phone Number Email Insurance Carrier Name Coverages Vision Policy Number Website Phone Number Email Client Manager Email Emergency Evacuation Carrier Name AIG Travel Guard – see policy above Coverage Emergency Evacuation Policy Number Phone Number Email Appendix 1: Internal Staff Message The following is a draft message that can be adjusted to better suit your team… Dear Staff, We have engaged Risk and Strategic Management [RSM] to support our ability to provide

chunks.heading: EMAIL NOTIFICATION DISTRIBUTIONS
chunks.text: timely and effective emergency support to staff during work activities and work-related travel for safety and security incidents. RSM will provide an emergency hotline number (This number can be called from any location; international roaming must be activated) with a 24/7 call center service, which is attached to this email – you should print and carry a copy of this with you. This service is only to be used for immediate and life-threatening situations, such as a natural disaster, if you have been seriously injured or require immediate hospitalization and need help if you have been involved in a serious accident if you have been robbed or assaulted, or during a civil disorder event or terrorist attack. If you are arrested during work abroad and cannot get through to your manager, you can use this hotline to activate a response. Please do not use this hotline for administrative purposes – such as if you lose your passport, miss your flight, or lose possessions. Such matters should be reported to your manager. Such matters should be reported through AIG Travel Guard at: Upon calling the hotline number, you will be asked for specific details, including: 1] your name, 2] the name of our organization, 3] how best to contact you, 4] where you are, 5] what is happening, and 6] if you are at immediate risk. Some basic immediate advice will be provided, and then a Risk Advisor from RSM will establish contact and offer more detailed advice. At the same time, the RSM Crisis Management Team will be activated to support you with the situation. If you have any questions relating to this service please contact: Danielle Martin at dmartin@partners.net and Natalie Abegesah at nabegesah@partners.net. If you have any questions relating to this service, please contact: Appendix 2: RSM Contact Numbers Area Name Title Phone Email Service Admin/Immediate Support Alan Rohn Director Risk Consulting & Resilience German Cell: +49 151 67970566 U.S. Cell +1 571 5602 389

documents.path: HR/Insurance & Benefits Information/SmartBenefits (Transit)/2012/SmartBenefits Overview.docx
documents.folder: HR/Insurance & Benefits Information/SmartBenefits (Transit)/2012

chunks.text:
Pre-Tax Transit and Parking Benefits
Transit benefits allow employees to deduct funds from their paycheck (before income and payroll taxes are applied), to be transferred to their SmarTrip card and used towards the actual cost of commuting via public transit.  
Offering transit benefits helps our employees save money on their commuting costs. It also helps our community by encouraging the use of public transit, thereby reducing traffic congestion and improving air quality.
Estimated Tax Savings  
SmartBenefits Deducted$1,500.00 ($125/month)
Federal Income Tax Saved– $375.00 (Assuming 25% tax rate)
State Income Tax Saved    –   $90.00  (Assuming 6% tax rate)
Employee FICA Saved – $114.75(Standard 7.65%)
Total Out-of-Pocket Cost  $ 920.25That’s a savings of almost 40% !!!!
IRS Regulations
Transit (metro, bus, etc.) = max. $125 per month, withheld pre-tax
Parking (at metro stations) = max. $240 per month, withheld pre-tax
SmartBenefits are not transferrable from one category to another
Unused SmartBenefits never expire 
Unused SmartBenefits cannot be refunded to the employee
SmartBenefits Overview
SmartBenefits can be used on any transit provider that allows payment using the SmarTrip card.
SmartBenefits can only be added to a registered SmarTrip card. Register yours online at: https://smartrip.wmata.com/Registration/Register.aspx   
Changes to SmartBenefits can be made before the 15th of the prior month.
Deductions are withheld from your paycheck pre-tax, and monthly benefit is split between the two paychecks that cover the benefit period. 
The full amount of your SmartBenefits are automatically loaded onto your SmarTrip on the 1st of each month. You can track your balances online.
Unused benefits will rollover each month into the same designation (transit/parking), up to the maximum capacity of the SmarTrip card.
If your Smartrip card is lost, damaged or stolen, immediately notify Metro by calling the (888) 762-7874 and have them deactivate your card to prevent anyone from using your funds. Once you have a replacement SmarTrip card, your benefits can be transferred to the new card.

documents.path: HR/Performance Management/2023 Performance template Review FO/USAID649_FO F2F Performance Eval Forms/2021 Performance Management Goal Setting Guidelines.docx
documents.folder: HR/Performance Management/2023 Performance template Review FO/USAID649_FO F2F Performance Eval Forms

chunks.text:
Performance Management: Goal Setting Guidelines
WHO? Every employee should agree on goals with their manager. Both participate actively. Managers may set goals for the employee if needed and employees may also set them proactively for themselves.
WHAT? 2-3 project related work goals. Goals are broad targets related directly to individual learning and job achievement. Goals are not tasks. Goals may connect to organizational strategy but do not always have to connect directly. A good goal is measurable but is not always easily measured numerically; some goals are more qualitative than quantitative. (e.g., “Improve morale in my team this year.” This could be measured with either team feedback or a reduction in % turnover.) Good goals are SMART:  
Specific
Measurable
Attainable
Relevant 
Time-bound
WHERE? Either in an online performance management system or any electronic method. Goal setting is about work planning, clarifying expectations, and communicating. As long as the goals and progress toward their achievement are documented, any method can work. 
WHEN? Before June 30, 2021. Goals may be carried forward, revised, added, or deleted anytime as needed. Our work is constantly changing and so are our goals. Performance is best assessed with continuous feedback throughout the year and official performance assessments at least every 6 months. 
WHY? Goal setting enables us to measure performance, demonstrate success, prioritize our work, delegate effectively, organize our teams, and grow professionally. 
HOW? For each goal, describe the end result and indicate quantity, quality, time frame, percentages, or other specific measures. Each goal should fit into and support the overall strategy of either the project/team or the employee’s professional learning goals. 
EXAMPLES:
Goal: 
Meet or exceed expectations for F2F host engagement 
Objectives:
Communicate X times a month with each of my assigned hosts
Assess hosts’ adopted recommendations each month for monitoring & tracking impact
Develop a minimum of X scopes of work per host that addresses their immediate needs

Goal 1: Expand the knowledge and use of virtual platforms
Leverage available technology to promote the work done with hosts and show the impact generated through the program.
Haven’t seen much of this effort throughout the year, but I have noticed communication with stakeholders via emails I’ve been copied in. To better meet this goal, can plan to provide already finalized content for the organization to publish in its online platforms.
Goal 2: Management of Field Office administrative activities
Create Calendar of contractual renewals for 2021-2022
Maintain current files on SharePoint.
Arrange in person meetings with USAID Mission and Peace Corps periodically
Michelle has done well in managing these administrative activities. Has looped me in on emails and set u p calls with USAID Mission and Peace Corps representatives and has been good at providing periodic updates to all to keep the communication flowing. 
Goal 3:Monitor agreed host recommendations against adopted host recommendations 
Work with staff to ensure that recommendations are achievable within given time frame
Check every three months in sessions with staff / hosts
Where not adopted, follow up with hosts to achieve 100% adoption
Where necessary, recheck recommendations / circumstances to ensure host is suitable of receiving F2F assistance
In follow up calls with Michelle, I’ve learned she has kept her team accountable in this area of recommendations. Still unclear how she has the team ensure the hosts are adopting at 100%

Professionalism – exudes great customer service responses and handles difficult situations very gracefully and humbly. 
Clear & Concise – delivers clear and well thought out insights when sharing innovative ideas or expressing opinions on a matter.
Follows instructions – captures and understands instructions with minimal follow up questions.
Team player – collaborates well with colleagues and contributes to the overall organization through her involvement in committees such as DEI and Wellness.

chunks.heading: DISCUSS OPPORTUNITIES FOR GROWTH
chunks.text:
Time Management – related to organizing priorities, is making sure external commitments not associated to work (ex: family care) do not interfere with time dedicated to work. 
Attention to Detail – has not spent sufficient time reviewing content from team members before it is passed on to headquarters. There are many errors, missing important information, and inconsistencies in versions of documents used for final presentation.

Michelle has done a great job and is eager to contribute in other ways. Just needs to be mindful of balancing priorities and managing time. Here is additional feedback from some of her colleagues:
Easy going and diplomatic
Knowledgeable in a wide range of topics
Sometimes needs to be more assertive 
Well organized
Keeps constant and full communication with teams
Provides clear and useful information
Proactive & innovative in problem solving

chunks.heading: TO THE SUPERVISOR
chunks.text: The evaluation will be considered confidential and should be discussed in detail with the employee. For the employee, supervisor, and POA to gain the most from this tool, supervisors are highly encouraged to take advantage of the 30,60,90 practice evaluation process to better engage with the employee. The 90-day evaluation must be completed and sent to HR for review. The 30-and 60-day evaluations are optional, though highly recommended, and not required to be sent to HR unless there is a performance problem documented. PLEASE NOTE: if there are performance issues, a 60-day evaluation will be required. FRAMEWORK PART 2: INTEGRATION PLAN (TALKING POINTS) for 90 DAYS Onboarding is an ongoing process. Even after the employee completes their initial training, keeping a close eye on them can drive integration to the team and culture, and a faster route to productivity. Follow through is critical. Start with the important connections the new hire must make in their first 30 days. Beyond these, other units that overlap, even infrequently, with the new hire or their team are important connections. A view of the organizational chart provides employees a sense of place within POA. Track initial assignments and milestones. What processes should the new hire have mastered; and by when. Communicate goals and projects and keep a checklist helps supervisors quantify whether the new hire’s development is on track with expectations. As supervisor measure goals and expectations against the checklist, you’ll know training is on track and new hires are getting the resources they need to succeed. Use the below integration plan as talking points during your one on one! This will ensure supervisors and new hire are on the same page and off to a great start at POA! 1-30 DAYS: GET TO KNOW THE ROLE & TEAM Review new hire probationary review evaluation Understand role/team/process/systems and requirements of the job. Meet with the team and units Ensure access to tools, people, process for long

of the job Start learning how your role connects with other units 61-90 DAYS: DEMOSTRATE PROGRESS Fully connects to teams, role Owning responsibility of actions and demonstrating ownership of role 90+ chunks.heading: AL SUPERVISOR
chunks.text:
La evaluación se considerará confidencial y debe discutirse en detalle con el empleado. 
Para que el empleado, el supervisor y el POA aprovechen al máximo esta herramienta, se recomienda encarecidamente a los supervisores que aprovechen el proceso de evaluación de práctica y realizar 2 evaluaciones de desempeño; una de ellas será verbal aproximadamente a la mitad del periodo de prueba y la segunda será por escrito al final del mismo.
La evaluación escrita debe completarse y enviarse a la Unidad de Recursos Humanos para su revisión. 
Se recomienda realizar evaluaciones de 30 y 90 días, son opcionales, aunque muy recomendables, y no es necesario enviarlas a Recursos Humanos a menos que haya un problema de rendimiento documentado. 
NOTA: si hay problemas de rendimiento, se requerirá una evaluación escrita y notificar a la unidad de recursos humanos.

chunks.heading: MARCO DE REFERENCIA
chunks.text: PARTE 2: PLAN DE INTEGRACIÓN (PUNTOS DE DISCUSIÓN) durante 90 DÍAS La incorporación es un proceso continuo. Incluso después de que el empleado complete su capacitación inicial, hacer seguimiento de cerca puede impulsar la integración con el equipo y la cultura, y una ruta más rápida hacia la productividad. El seguimiento es fundamental. Comience con las conexiones importantes que el nuevo empleado debe hacer en sus primeros 30 días. Más allá de estos, otras unidades que se superponen, incluso con poca frecuencia, con el nuevo empleado o su equipo son conexiones importantes. Una vista del organigrama proporciona a los empleados un sentido de lugar dentro de POA. Asignaciones e hitos iniciales del rack T. Qué procesos debe haber dominado el nuevo empleado; y para cuándo. Comunicar los resultados y proyectos y mantener una lista de verificación ayuda a los supervisores a cuantificar si el desarrollo de la nueva contratación está en el buen camino con las expectativas. A medida que el supervisor mida los objetivos y las expectativas en comparación con la lista de verificación, sabrá que la capacitación está en camino y que las nuevas contrataciones están obteniendo los recursos que necesitan para tener éxito. ¡Utilice el siguiente plan de integración como puntos de conversación durante su uno a uno! ¡Esto asegurará que los supervisores y los nuevos empleados estén en la misma página y tengan un gran comienzo en POA! 1-30 DÍAS: CONOZCA EL ROL Y EL EQUIPO Revisar la evaluación de revisión de periodo de prueba de nuevos empleados Comprender el rol/equipo/proceso/sistemas y los requisitos del trabajo. Reúnase con el equipo y las unidades Garantizar el acceso a herramientas, personas y procesos para el éxito a largo plazo Capacitaciones estándar de incorporación completas Establecer reuniones semanales / quincenales 31-60 DAYS: AUTÓNOMOS EN SU PAPEL Demostrar comprensión de las herramientas, el proceso y los requisitos del trabajo Comience a aprender cómo su rol se conecta

chunks.heading: MARCO DE REFERENCIA
chunks.text: con otras unidades 61-90 DAYS: DEMOSTRAR EL PROGRESO Se conecta completamente con los equipos, el rol Asumir la responsabilidad de las acciones y demostrar la propiedad del rol 90+ DAYS: Establecer objetivos de rendimiento mediante la revisión anual del desempeño PARTE 3: REVISIÓN DEL SUPERVISOR Factores y descripción del desempeño laboral Calificación y comentarios del supervisor Marque una casilla Cumple No cumple No observado Productividad Demuestra compromiso con la producción de trabajo que cumple con los estándares / objetivos de la unidad/programa, las políticas de POA, garantiza la consistencia y la precisión en (resultados/ entregables, etc.) Iniciativa Muestra una gran energía para abordar los desafíos relacionados con las tareas asignadas, demuestra responsabilidad por el propio aprendizaje, funciona bien sin ninguna supervisión Habilidades Técnicas Demuestra y posee las habilidades necesarias para desempeñarse Habilidades de supervisión Si aplica Motiva a los empleados, delegando eficazmente las actividades de los equipos, selecciona el canal de comunicación más eficaz y resuelve los conflictos Confianza Demuestra asistencia satisfactoria, reportes de trabajo y reuniones según lo programado Cooperativismo Trabaja bien con los demás, la voluntad de compartir experiencia e información con otros demuestra una actitud colaborativa Comunicación Demuestra fuertes habilidades verbales y escritas Adaptabilidad Actúa constructivamente y realiza ajustes de la retroalimentación o el cambio, se desempeña bajo presión, maneja múltiples tareas, etc. Diversidad, equidad e inclusión Fomenta y contribuye a un ambiente de trabajo que es acogedor para todos, trata a todas las personas con respeto, cortesía y dignidad.
